q: You need to store orders and their line items together in Azure Cosmos DB for NoSQL. Both entity types are accessed together in 90% of reads. How do you model this?
a: Store multiple entity types in the same container by using a 'type' discriminator property on each document. Orders and their line items share the same partition key (e.g., orderId), so they land in the same logical partition and can be fetched in a single point read or low-cost single-partition query. This eliminates cross-partition joins that NoSQL does not natively support. You define a 'type' field (e.g., 'order' vs 'lineItem') so application code can distinguish entities after retrieval. The container-level design colocates entities without a relational JOIN, trading normalized schema for read efficiency. This pattern is the standard Cosmos DB NoSQL approach when entities have a clear parent-child relationship and are queried together.
fj: Use separate containers per entity type so that schema changes to one type don't affect the other.
fs: Separate containers force cross-container queries (not natively supported) and prevent transactional batch operations across entity types. Colocating related entity types in one container using a type discriminator is the recommended Cosmos DB pattern for tightly related entities.

q: When should you embed related entities inside a single Cosmos DB document versus referencing them across documents?
a: Embed when the related data is always read together, the child data has bounded growth (e.g., an order with up to 10 line items), and the combined document stays well under the 2 MB item limit. Embedding eliminates extra round trips and enables atomic updates in a single replace operation. Reference (store only the id of the related entity) when the child collection is unbounded (e.g., all tweets by a user), the child is frequently updated independently, or the child is shared across multiple parents. Referencing keeps individual documents small and focused but requires additional point reads or queries to hydrate the full object graph. A practical heuristic: embed when you read the relationship 1:1 with the parent; reference when the cardinality is 1:many and the 'many' side grows without bound or is accessed independently.
fj: Always embed related entities to avoid extra reads and improve performance.
fs: Embedding unbounded child collections causes documents to grow beyond the 2 MB item limit and makes partial updates expensive. Referencing is the right choice for unbounded one-to-many or many-to-many relationships.

q: A product catalog item embeds the category name. The category is renamed. How do you handle denormalized data updates in Cosmos DB?
a: Denormalization trades write complexity for read performance: the category name is duplicated inside every product document to avoid joins. When the category is renamed you must update every product document that references it. The recommended pattern is to drive these updates from the Cosmos DB change feed: a category update triggers a change feed event, which an Azure Function (or change feed processor) reads to fan out patch operations against all affected product documents. Alternatively, if consistency can tolerate eventual propagation, use a background job or Azure Data Factory pipeline. For tightly consistent requirements, consider storing a categoryId reference and resolving the name at read time — a hybrid embedding strategy. Key trade-off: denormalized reads are fast and cheap (single document), but denormalized writes require coordinated fan-out updates.
fj: Denormalized data automatically stays in sync in Cosmos DB because the database propagates updates to embedded copies.
fs: Cosmos DB does not maintain referential integrity or cascade updates. Denormalized copies are independent JSON fields; keeping them in sync is entirely the application's responsibility, typically via change feed processors.

q: What is a reference-based document design in Cosmos DB and when is it preferable to embedding?
a: In a reference-based design each document stores only the identifier (e.g., userId, productId) of related entities rather than the full entity data. The application issues additional point reads or queries to resolve those identifiers. This is preferable when: (1) the referenced entity is updated frequently and embedding would require widespread document updates; (2) the referenced entity is large and including it would push documents close to the 2 MB limit; (3) the same entity is referenced by many parents (many-to-many). The cost is additional RU charges for each extra read and increased round-trip latency. Cosmos DB point reads (ReadItem by partition key + id) cost 1 RU for a 1 KB document, so resolving a handful of references is affordable, but resolving dozens per request accumulates latency and RU cost quickly.
fj: Reference-based designs are cheaper in RU cost because each document is smaller.
fs: Smaller individual documents reduce per-document read cost, but referencing requires multiple reads to assemble a full object graph. The total RU cost for a page of data often exceeds that of a single embedded read, making embedding cheaper for frequently co-accessed data.

q: How do you choose a partition key for an Azure Cosmos DB for NoSQL container, and what properties make a partition key ideal?
a: A good partition key has high cardinality (many distinct values), distributes both data storage and request throughput evenly across logical partitions, and aligns with the most common query predicates so most queries are single-partition. Each logical partition is capped at 20 GB; if any single value would exceed that, the key is too coarse. Avoid 'hot' partition keys where a small number of values receive the vast majority of requests (e.g., a status field with only 'active'/'closed'). The partition key is immutable after creation, so it cannot be changed without a container rebuild. The partition key must be present on every item. The 'id' field is valid but makes every write a cross-partition query when filtering by non-id attributes. For transactional batches and stored procedures, all items involved must share the same partition key value.
fj: Choosing 'id' as the partition key is ideal because every item has a unique id, guaranteeing even distribution.
fs: While 'id' gives perfect cardinality, it means every query that filters on any other attribute must fan out across all partitions. Partition key selection must optimize for actual query patterns, not just distribution.

q: Walk through how to identify access patterns and map them to a Cosmos DB data model before writing any code.
a: Start by listing every read and write operation the application will perform, noting: the filter predicates, the data returned, the frequency (reads/sec, writes/sec), and whether the operation is latency-sensitive. Group operations by what data they access together — items co-accessed by the same query should ideally share a partition key value. Identify the most frequent and latency-sensitive read as your 'primary access pattern' and design the partition key and document shape to serve it with a single-partition query or point read. Secondary access patterns can be served with cross-partition queries (higher RU cost) or secondary indexes. For write-heavy patterns, minimize document size and avoid large embedded arrays that cause frequent full-document rewrites. Document the access patterns in a table (operation, filter, sort, RU estimate) before finalizing the schema — changes after data is loaded are expensive because the partition key is immutable.
fj: You can optimize the data model iteratively after deployment since Cosmos DB allows schema changes at any time.
fs: While Cosmos DB is schema-flexible (you can add fields freely), the partition key is fixed at container creation. Changing the access-pattern-driven partition key requires creating a new container and migrating all data — a costly operation in production. Access pattern analysis must precede modeling.

q: How do you configure a default TTL on a Cosmos DB container and how does TTL interact with individual document-level overrides?
a: TTL (Time-to-Live) is set as a container-level property named 'defaultTtl' in seconds. Setting it to -1 enables TTL on the container but does not expire items unless each item sets its own 'ttl' property. Setting it to a positive integer (e.g., 86400 for 24 hours) expires all items after that duration from their last modified time unless a document's own 'ttl' property overrides it. A document-level 'ttl' of -1 makes that specific item immortal even if the container default would expire it. TTL expiration is a background server-side process that runs at low priority and does not consume provisioned throughput (RU). Deleted items consume change feed events. You configure default TTL via the Azure portal (Container Settings > Time-to-Live), ARM template, or SDK (ContainerProperties.DefaultTimeToLive).
fj: Setting defaultTtl on the container immediately starts expiring all existing items after the specified duration.
fs: TTL is measured from each item's _ts (last-modified timestamp), not from when TTL was enabled on the container. Existing items only expire once the time since their _ts exceeds the TTL value. Items with an explicit ttl=-1 are never expired regardless of the container default.

q: Describe a document versioning strategy for Cosmos DB that supports reading historical states and migrating schemas without downtime.
a: Add a 'schemaVersion' integer property to every document (start at 1). When a schema change is needed: (1) increment schemaVersion in all new writes; (2) apply lazy migration — when the application reads an old-version document it upgrades it in-memory and writes back the new version, so the migration is incremental rather than requiring a batch job. For full audit history, use the 'snapshot' pattern: on each update, write the new state with the canonical id and archive the previous state as a new item with a versioned id (e.g., 'orderId-v2'). The change feed can also drive a separate audit container that appends every change. For point-in-time recovery, continuous backup or the change feed history are the platform-level alternatives. Using 'schemaVersion' lets the application use a migration chain (v1→v2→v3) so code handles multiple versions without breaking existing deployed instances.
fj: Cosmos DB handles schema versioning automatically because it is schema-less.
fs: Schema-less means Cosmos DB imposes no structure — it does not manage versions, validate shapes, or migrate old documents. Application code is entirely responsible for handling versioning and backward compatibility across schema changes.

code.label: Schema versioning pattern

q: Your Cosmos DB container stores multi-tenant data. Tenants vary widely in size. How do you choose a partition key that supports transactions while avoiding hot partitions?
a: For multi-tenant workloads with skewed tenant sizes, a single-level partition key of tenantId creates hot partitions for large tenants. The recommended approach is a hierarchical partition key with tenantId as the first level and userId (or entityId) as the second level. This keeps all data for a tenant in the same physical partition set (enabling efficient tenant-scoped queries) while distributing individual items across many logical partitions to avoid the 20 GB per-logical-partition cap. Transactional Batch operations require all items to share the exact same full partition key value; with hierarchical keys this means items must share all levels, not just the first. If truly atomic multi-item transactions are required only within a single user's partition, design accordingly. For cross-user transactions within a tenant, stored procedures scoped to a single partition key value remain the only server-side transaction mechanism.
fj: Use a composite synthetic key like 'tenantId_userId' to handle large tenants, as it distributes data identically to hierarchical partition keys.
fs: Synthetic keys concatenate values into a single string partition key, which means Cosmos DB treats the combined value as one opaque key. Hierarchical partition keys are superior because they allow prefix-based query routing (tenant-scoped queries hit only the relevant subset of physical partitions), which synthetic keys do not support.

q: What is the RU cost impact of a cross-partition query versus a single-partition query in Cosmos DB, and when is cross-partition acceptable?
a: A single-partition query routes to exactly one physical partition and pays RU cost proportional to the data scanned and returned within that partition. A cross-partition query fans out to every physical partition, executes in parallel, and charges the sum of RU across all partitions — often 10–100x more expensive for large containers. Cross-partition queries also consume gateway resources to merge results and may surface higher tail latency. Cross-partition is acceptable when: (1) the query is infrequent and analytics-oriented (not on the hot path); (2) the result set is small (high selectivity via index use); (3) the workload uses autoscale throughput and occasional spikes are acceptable. To evaluate cost, examine the 'x-ms-request-charge' response header or the 'RequestCharge' property in the SDK response. The Data Explorer in the portal shows RU cost per query execution.
fj: Cross-partition queries always have the same RU cost as single-partition queries because Cosmos DB parallelizes them efficiently.
fs: Parallelism reduces latency but not RU cost — RU charges accumulate across every partition scanned. A cross-partition query on a 100-partition container can cost 100x a single-partition query for the same data volume.

q: How do you calculate and evaluate throughput and data storage distribution based on partition key selection for a Cosmos DB container?
a: Throughput distribution is evaluated by estimating the percentage of reads and writes that will target each unique partition key value. If 80% of requests go to 5% of partition key values, those are hot partitions that will throttle even if total provisioned RU is sufficient. Data distribution is evaluated by estimating the size of data per partition key value; each logical partition is capped at 20 GB. Tools: (1) Azure Monitor's 'Normalized RU Consumption' metric broken down by PhysicalPartitionId reveals current hot-partition imbalance; (2) the 'PartitionKeyStatistics' in the SDK (FeedResponse on a container.GetPartitionKeyRangesAsync call) shows key range distribution; (3) Data Explorer's partition key advisor gives a pre-creation recommendation. The goal is a coefficient of variation less than ~20% across partitions for both data volume and RU consumption.
fj: As long as total provisioned RU is sufficient for the workload, partition key distribution does not matter.
fs: Cosmos DB enforces per-partition throughput limits. Even with generous total RU provisioning, a hot logical partition that receives more requests than its share of physical partition RU capacity will be throttled (HTTP 429), causing failed requests and retries regardless of overall RU availability.

q: What is a synthetic partition key in Cosmos DB, when do you construct one, and how is it implemented?
a: A synthetic partition key is an application-constructed field that concatenates two or more natural properties into a single string, used when no single natural field has sufficient cardinality or distributes data evenly. For example, if userId alone causes 20 GB logical partition overflow but the combination of userId + month achieves good distribution, you build a synthetic key 'userId_2024-06'. The field is computed by the application at write time and stored explicitly on the document — Cosmos DB treats it as any other property. The synthetic key must be present on every document before insertion. One downside compared to hierarchical partition keys: prefix queries (e.g., all data for a userId regardless of month) become cross-partition queries because the composite string is opaque. Hierarchical partition keys are preferred when prefix routing is needed; synthetic keys remain useful when you have exactly two properties with predictable concatenation patterns.
fj: A synthetic partition key is automatically computed by Cosmos DB from multiple document fields you specify at container creation.
fs: Cosmos DB does not compute or store synthetic keys — the application must calculate and write the combined value to a dedicated field on every document before upsert. There is no server-side formula engine for partition keys.

q: What is a hierarchical partition key in Cosmos DB, what problem does it solve, and how do you define it?
a: A hierarchical partition key (subpartitioning) allows up to three levels of partition key, defined as an ordered list of paths (e.g., ['/tenantId', '/userId', '/sessionId']). It solves the problem where a single-level key (tenantId) creates logical partitions that exceed 20 GB for large tenants, while a finer key (userId) forces all tenant-scoped queries to be cross-partition. With hierarchical keys, Cosmos DB collocates documents sharing the same first-level value on the same physical partition set, enabling efficient prefix queries (WHERE tenantId = 'T1') while allowing the data to spread across many logical partitions within that physical partition. To define it, use the SDK's PartitionKeyDefinition with SubpartitioningVersion, or pass the paths array to the container creation API. It is strongly recommended to add the item id as the last level to ensure logical partition size never exceeds 20 GB.
fj: Hierarchical partition keys allow you to query by any level independently with equal efficiency.
fs: Hierarchical partition key queries are efficient only when filtering from the first level down (prefix queries). Filtering by only the second or third level without the first level results in a cross-partition fan-out, same as any non-partition-key filter.

q: How do you evaluate throughput and storage requirements for a Cosmos DB workload, and what provisioning mode should you choose?
a: Estimate RU requirements by profiling representative operations: a point read of a 1 KB item costs ~1 RU; writes cost ~5 RU; complex queries vary. Multiply per-operation RU by peak operations-per-second to get peak RU/s. Add ~20% headroom. For storage, estimate items × average item size; remember that indexing adds roughly 10–20% overhead by default. Provisioning modes: (1) Manual throughput — fixed RU/s you set, billed hourly; best when load is predictable and consistently high. (2) Autoscale — set a max RU/s; Cosmos scales down to 10% of max automatically; billed at the highest RU used in each hour; best for variable or spiky workloads. (3) Serverless — billed per actual RU consumed with no provisioned throughput; best for dev/test or sporadic workloads up to ~5000 RU/s peak. Database-level shared throughput can spread a single RU budget across up to 25 containers.
fj: Autoscale always costs less than manual throughput because Cosmos scales down automatically during low traffic.
fs: Autoscale's minimum is 10% of the configured max, and the hourly bill uses the peak RU reached in that hour — not the average. For a consistently busy container at or near max, manual throughput is cheaper. Autoscale saves cost only when there is meaningful variation between peak and off-peak.

q: When should you use database-level provisioned throughput in Cosmos DB rather than container-level throughput?
a: Database-level provisioned throughput (shared throughput) allows up to 25 containers in the same database to share a single RU/s pool. Use it when: (1) you have many small containers whose individual peaks do not overlap in time (the shared pool serves them collectively); (2) you want to minimize minimum billing — a shared 400 RU/s database costs the same as one 400 RU/s container while serving multiple containers; (3) containers have uneven, temporally staggered load. Do not use shared throughput when any single container regularly needs more than the database's total RU (it will starve others), or when containers have consistently high and simultaneous demand. Containers without dedicated throughput cannot exceed their share of the database's provisioned RU. Containers can be assigned dedicated throughput at any time, opting out of the shared pool for that container.
fj: Database-level throughput is always more cost-effective than per-container throughput because the RU pool is shared.
fs: Shared throughput only saves cost when containers' peaks do not coincide. If multiple containers simultaneously need high throughput, the shared pool creates contention and throttling. Per-container throughput guarantees isolation and predictable performance.

q: How do you configure throughput for a Cosmos DB container in the Azure portal, including switching between manual and autoscale?
a: In the Azure portal, navigate to your Cosmos DB account → Data Explorer → expand your database → select the container → click 'Settings'. Under 'Throughput' you see the current mode (Manual or Autoscale) and the RU/s value. To change throughput, edit the number in the input field and click 'Save'. To switch between Manual and Autoscale, click the toggle and confirm the migration — this is non-destructive and takes effect immediately. For autoscale, the portal shows the 'Max RU/s' field; the system scales between 10% and 100% of that value. You can also configure throughput at database creation or at container creation time. The portal shows the estimated cost per hour based on current settings. For analytical store-enabled containers, there is a separate column store that does not consume provisioned RU.
fj: You must use the CLI or SDK to switch between manual and autoscale throughput; the Azure portal only supports reading current throughput settings.
fs: The Azure portal fully supports switching between manual and autoscale throughput through the container's Settings pane. The CLI and SDKs also support this, but the portal is a valid and commonly used method.

q: Compare Gateway mode and Direct mode connectivity in the Cosmos DB .NET SDK. When should you choose each?
a: In Gateway mode the SDK connects to Cosmos DB's front-end gateway nodes over HTTPS (port 443). The gateway routes requests to the appropriate backend nodes. Gateway mode works through all network environments including corporate proxies and firewalls, uses fewer ports, but adds an extra network hop (~1–2 ms latency overhead) and is slightly lower throughput. In Direct mode the SDK discovers partition-to-node mappings via the gateway, then connects directly to backend nodes over TCP (HTTPS is also supported but TCP is default). Direct mode removes the gateway hop, delivering lower latency (often 1–3 ms less) and higher throughput. The integrated cache (dedicated gateway) is only available in Gateway mode. Choose Gateway mode for: applications behind strict firewalls, Azure Functions/serverless (connection limits matter), or when using the integrated cache. Choose Direct mode for: latency-sensitive workloads, high-throughput applications, and when network topology allows direct TCP connections.
fj: Direct mode is always faster and should be used for all production workloads.
fs: Direct mode requires that TCP ports to Cosmos DB backend nodes are open through firewalls and proxies. In restricted enterprise networks or when using the integrated cache, Gateway mode is required. The performance difference is also small enough that Gateway mode is acceptable for most workloads.

code.label: Configuring connectivity mode in .NET SDK

// Gateway mode (use for proxies, firewalls, integrated cache)

q: How do you connect an application to a Cosmos DB emulator for offline development, and what are the emulator's known limitations?
a: The Azure Cosmos DB emulator runs locally and exposes the same API surface as the cloud service. The default endpoint is https://localhost:8081 with a well-known key starting with 'C2y6yD…'. Connect using the same SDK as production; only the endpoint and key differ. The emulator certificate is self-signed, so disable SSL verification in development (set HttpClientFactory to bypass SSL or import the emulator certificate). Known limitations: the emulator supports only a single fixed account; it does not replicate multi-region behavior; serverless, analytical store, and some newer features are not supported; performance is single-node and not representative of cloud throughput. Use environment-based configuration (e.g., COSMOS_ENDPOINT and COSMOS_KEY env vars) to switch between emulator and cloud without code changes. The emulator Docker image (mcr.microsoft.com/cosmosdb/linux/azure-cosmos-emulator) is cross-platform.
fj: The Cosmos DB emulator is a full replica of the cloud service and can be used for performance benchmarking.
fs: The emulator is a single-node process that does not replicate multi-region replication, partitioning behavior, or cloud-scale throughput. Benchmarking against the emulator produces misleading results; use a real Cosmos DB account for performance testing.

code.label: Connecting to Cosmos DB emulator (Python SDK)
lines: connection_verify=False   # bypass self-signed cert in dev only

q: How do you handle transient connection errors in Cosmos DB SDK clients, and why should you use a singleton client?
a: The Cosmos DB SDK implements automatic retry logic for transient errors (HTTP 429 rate limiting, 503 service unavailable) with exponential back-off. You can customize retry behavior via CosmosClientOptions.MaxRetryAttemptsOnRateLimitedRequests (default: 9) and MaxRetryWaitTimeOnRateLimitedRequests (default: 30 seconds). For non-retryable errors (400 bad request, 404 not found, 409 conflict) you must handle them in application code. Use a singleton CosmosClient for the lifetime of your application: the client maintains a connection pool and caches partition key routing maps. Creating a new client per request is extremely expensive — it forces DNS resolution, TCP connection establishment, and partition routing discovery on every call, which adds hundreds of milliseconds of latency. In .NET dependency injection, register CosmosClient as a singleton. In Azure Functions, use a static field to hold the client instance.
fj: Creating a new CosmosClient per request is acceptable because the SDK caches connection state internally.
fs: The SDK caches state within a single client instance, not across instances. Each new CosmosClient performs expensive initialization (connection handshake, routing map download). Creating one per request causes massive latency overhead and may exhaust TCP socket limits.

code.label: Singleton CosmosClient in .NET DI

q: Write a Cosmos DB SQL query that uses arrays, nested object access, aggregation, and ORDER BY. Explain each clause.
a: Cosmos DB SQL (NoSQL API query language) supports dot notation for nested object access, the ARRAY_CONTAINS function for array membership tests, aggregate functions (COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX), and ORDER BY with optional ASC/DESC. Nested property access uses standard dot notation (c.address.city). Arrays can be iterated with the JOIN keyword for unnesting or tested with ARRAY_CONTAINS. Aggregates require either a single-partition query or a cross-partition query with EnableCrossPartitionQuery = true. ORDER BY on non-partition-key properties requires a range index on that property (which the default indexing policy includes for all scalar properties). GROUP BY is supported for computing aggregates per group but requires the result to fit within a single response page.
fj: Cosmos DB SQL queries support standard SQL JOINs between containers, similar to relational databases.
fs: Cosmos DB SQL JOIN only operates within a single document to unnest arrays into a flat result set. There is no JOIN between two separate containers — that is a fundamental NoSQL design constraint. Cross-document joins must be handled in application code.

code.label: Arrays, nested objects, aggregation, ORDER BY

q: What is a correlated subquery in Cosmos DB SQL and when would you use one?
a: A correlated subquery in Cosmos DB SQL is a subquery that references properties from the outer query's FROM clause. The most common use is to flatten arrays: instead of a JOIN (which can produce duplicate outer rows), you use EXISTS or a scalar subquery to filter based on array content without unnesting. The EXISTS subquery returns true if any element in the inner result set satisfies the predicate, avoiding cartesian expansion. Scalar subqueries (e.g., SELECT VALUE in a subquery) return a single value computed per outer row. Correlated subqueries are useful for: (1) testing array element conditions without unnesting; (2) computing a derived value per document; (3) filtering based on nested object conditions. They execute within a single document's scope — they cannot reference other containers.
fj: A correlated subquery in Cosmos DB can reference data from a different container to perform lookups.
fs: Cosmos DB subqueries are scoped entirely to a single document. They can reference arrays and nested objects within that document but have no mechanism to look up data from another container. Cross-container lookups must be performed in application code.

code.label: Correlated subquery with EXISTS
lines: -- Return orders that have at least one line item over $100

q: How do you use array functions and type-checking functions in Cosmos DB SQL queries?
a: Cosmos DB SQL provides array functions: ARRAY_CONTAINS(arr, val [, partial]) tests membership; ARRAY_LENGTH(arr) returns element count; ARRAY_SLICE(arr, start, length) extracts a sub-array; ARRAY_CONCAT concatenates arrays. Type-checking functions determine a property's JSON type at query time: IS_STRING, IS_NUMBER, IS_BOOL, IS_NULL, IS_DEFINED, IS_ARRAY, IS_OBJECT. IS_DEFINED(c.optionalField) is essential for schema-flexible containers where not all documents have the same fields — filtering without IS_DEFINED on optional fields can silently exclude items where the property is absent. ARRAY_CONTAINS with partial=true does a subset match on objects within the array. These functions are scalar and can appear in WHERE, SELECT, or ORDER BY clauses.
fj: IS_NULL(c.field) returns true both when the field is absent from a document and when it is explicitly set to null.
fs: IS_NULL returns true only when the field exists and its value is the JSON null literal. IS_DEFINED returns true when the field exists (regardless of value). IS_NULL(c.x) on a document without the 'x' property returns false — you need IS_DEFINED(c.x) = false to detect absent fields.

q: When should you use a point operation versus a query operation in Cosmos DB, and what is the RU cost difference?
a: A point operation (ReadItem) retrieves a single item by its partition key + id combination in O(1) cost: approximately 1 RU for a 1 KB item. A query operation scans the index and returns a result set; even a query that returns a single item by partition key + id costs slightly more than a point read because it involves query parsing and index traversal. Use point operations whenever you have both the partition key value and the item id — they are the most efficient operation in Cosmos DB. Use queries when you are filtering by non-id properties, need multiple items, or are performing aggregations. The SDK's ReadItemAsync (point read) is semantically different from GetItemQueryIterator with a SELECT WHERE id = filter — always prefer ReadItemAsync when you have both key components.
fj: A single-item query filtered by partition key and id is equivalent in RU cost to a point read.
fs: A filtered query incurs query overhead (parsing, index lookup, result formatting) even when it returns exactly one item. Benchmarks show single-item queries cost ~2.3 RU versus ~1 RU for a point read. Always use ReadItemAsync when both partition key and id are known.

"SELECT * FROM c WHERE c.id = 'ord-001' AND c.pk = 'ord-001'"

q: How do you implement create, replace, and delete point operations in Cosmos DB using the SDK?
a: The Cosmos DB SDK provides distinct methods for each point operation: CreateItemAsync creates a new item and fails with 409 Conflict if an item with the same id already exists in the partition. UpsertItemAsync creates or replaces — it succeeds whether the item exists or not. ReplaceItemAsync replaces an existing item and fails with 404 if it does not exist; it can optionally enforce ETag-based optimistic concurrency via ItemRequestOptions.IfMatchEtag. DeleteItemAsync removes an item by id + partition key; it returns 204 No Content on success and 404 if the item does not exist. PatchItemAsync applies partial updates without a full document replace. Each operation returns an ItemResponse containing the resulting item (except delete), the request charge, and ETag. Always include the partition key as a PartitionKey parameter, not as a query filter.
fj: UpsertItemAsync is identical to CreateItemAsync except it never returns a 409 error.
fs: Upsert replaces the entire document if it exists, while create preserves the existing document on conflict. For idempotent pipelines upsert is useful, but for strict insert-only semantics (e.g., deduplication), use CreateItemAsync and catch the 409 to detect duplicates.

code.label: Create, replace, delete point operations

q: How does the Patch operation work in Cosmos DB and what patch operation types are supported?
a: PatchItemAsync applies granular partial updates to an existing document server-side without fetching the full item first. Supported patch operations: Add (add or replace a property), Set (same as Add for scalars), Remove (delete a property or array element), Replace (replace an existing property — fails if absent, unlike Set), Increment (atomically add a numeric delta), Move (move a property to a new path). Operations are listed in a PatchOperations list and applied atomically in order. Patch costs fewer RU than a read-modify-write cycle for large documents because it does not read the full item. Use Patch for: incrementing counters, adding new fields without full replacement, removing sensitive data, and updating nested properties without overwriting siblings. Filter predicates can be added (PatchItemOptions.FilterPredicate) to conditionally apply the patch based on a WHERE clause.
fj: PatchItemAsync reads the full document, applies changes locally, and writes it back — equivalent to a replace.
fs: PatchItemAsync is a native server-side operation; the server applies the patch operations directly to the stored document without sending the full document to the client. This reduces network bandwidth and RU cost, especially for large documents with small updates.

code.label: Patch operation — increment counter + add field

q: How does TransactionalBatch work in Cosmos DB SDK, what are its constraints, and when should you use it?
a: TransactionalBatch executes a group of point operations (create, read, replace, upsert, delete, patch) atomically against a single partition key value — all operations succeed or all are rolled back. It is the only way to achieve ACID transactions across multiple items in Cosmos DB for NoSQL without a stored procedure. Constraints: all items must share the same partition key value; the batch payload cannot exceed 2 MB; maximum 100 operations per batch; each operation must complete within a 5-second execution window. The response includes per-operation status codes and a batch-level success/failure indicator. Use TransactionalBatch for: inventory reservations, ledger debits/credits, multi-document state transitions within a partition. Avoid for cross-partition atomic operations — these are not supported natively.
fj: TransactionalBatch can span multiple partition key values as long as all items are in the same container.
fs: TransactionalBatch is strictly scoped to a single partition key value. Cosmos DB's ACID guarantee for server-side transactions is bounded to a single logical partition. Cross-partition atomic operations are not supported; application-level saga/compensation patterns are required instead.

code.label: TransactionalBatch for debit + credit

q: How do you perform a bulk load in Cosmos DB using the SDK's Bulk Support, and what configuration is required?
a: Bulk support in the .NET SDK v3+ is enabled by setting CosmosClientOptions.AllowBulkExecution = true. When enabled, the SDK internally batches concurrent operations into efficient server-side bulk calls, maximizing throughput by coalescing requests and optimizing connection reuse. Instead of awaiting each operation, you fire off all operations as concurrent tasks and await Task.WhenAll to collect results. This allows the SDK to pack operations into Direct-mode TCP streams efficiently. Best practices: use UpsertItemAsync for idempotent loads; handle per-item failures from the task results rather than a batch-level exception; set MaxConcurrency to a high value (e.g., -1 or 100) to let the SDK saturate available throughput. Bulk support is ideal for initial data loading, migration, and high-volume ETL writes. The dedicated Cosmos DB Bulk Executor library (older) has been superseded by this SDK-native bulk support.
fj: Bulk support in the SDK submits all items in a single API call, similar to a SQL BULK INSERT.
fs: SDK bulk support does not use a single bulk API endpoint. It optimizes concurrency by coalescing many parallel individual operations into efficient batches internally, but each operation still goes through the normal CRUD path. There is no single-call 'BULK INSERT' equivalent in the Cosmos DB NoSQL API.

code.label: Bulk upsert with AllowBulkExecution

q: How do you implement optimistic concurrency control in Cosmos DB using ETags?
a: Every Cosmos DB item has a system-generated '_etag' property that changes on every write. To perform optimistic concurrency: read the item, capture its _etag, then on the subsequent write include IfMatchEtag in ItemRequestOptions. If another writer has modified the item since your read, its _etag will differ from yours, and Cosmos DB returns HTTP 412 (Precondition Failed). Your application must then retry the read-modify-write cycle with the fresh _etag. This prevents lost-update conflicts without pessimistic locking, which does not exist in Cosmos DB. ETags work for ReplaceItemAsync, UpsertItemAsync, PatchItemAsync, and DeleteItemAsync. They can also be used in TransactionalBatch per-operation via BatchItemRequestOptions.IfMatchEtag. ETag-based concurrency is the canonical mechanism for concurrent editors on the same document.
fj: Cosmos DB uses row-level locking to prevent concurrent updates, so optimistic concurrency is only needed for special cases.
fs: Cosmos DB does not support pessimistic locking. All items are always writable by any concurrent client. ETags provide the only built-in mechanism for detecting and handling write conflicts — optimistic concurrency is the standard pattern for Cosmos DB.

code.label: Optimistic concurrency with ETag

q: How do you override the default consistency level for individual Cosmos DB read requests using query request options?
a: The account-level default consistency can be overridden to a weaker level per request (you cannot strengthen beyond the account default). Set ConsistencyLevel in QueryRequestOptions or ItemRequestOptions to Eventual, ConsistentPrefix, Session, or BoundedStaleness (only weaker levels work). Overriding to Eventual for read-heavy reporting queries reduces RU cost and latency compared to Session or Strong. The account-level setting is the ceiling; per-request overrides must be equal to or weaker than that ceiling. Setting a stronger level (e.g., requesting Strong on an Eventual account) throws an error. Override syntax in .NET: new QueryRequestOptions { ConsistencyLevel = ConsistencyLevel.Eventual }. In Python: cosmos_client.read_item(..., consistency_level='Eventual').
fj: You can override the consistency level per request to any level including Strong, regardless of the account default.
fs: Per-request consistency can only be set to a level equal to or weaker than the account default. Requesting a stronger level than the account supports results in an error. Consistency level can never be strengthened at request scope.

code.label: Override consistency to Eventual for a query

q: What is session consistency in Cosmos DB and how do you propagate session tokens to maintain read-your-own-writes guarantees across client instances?
a: Session consistency guarantees that a single client session reads its own writes and observes a monotonically increasing sequence of updates. The SDK automatically manages the session token for requests made through the same CosmosClient instance. However, when multiple client instances (e.g., multiple Azure Function instances, microservices) need to share session context — such as after a write in one instance being read in another — you must manually propagate the session token. Extract the session token from the write response (response.Headers.Session or response.SessionToken), pass it to the reader via an application mechanism (HTTP header, message queue), and supply it in ItemRequestOptions.SessionToken on the read request. Without token propagation, different client instances have independent session states and may read stale data from a different instance.
fj: Session consistency automatically guarantees read-your-own-writes across all instances of your application without any manual token management.
fs: Session tokens are per-CosmosClient-instance state. Different application instances (e.g., separate containers in a microservice deployment) do not share session context automatically. Propagating the session token explicitly is required to guarantee read-your-own-writes across independent client instances.

q: How do you write, deploy, and call a stored procedure in Cosmos DB for NoSQL?
a: Stored procedures in Cosmos DB are JavaScript functions that execute server-side within a single partition key value. They receive a context object that exposes collection.readDocument, createDocument, replaceDocument, deleteDocument, and queryDocuments. Write the procedure as a JS function; deploy it via the SDK (Scripts.CreateStoredProcedureAsync with StoredProcedureProperties containing the JS body) or the Azure portal's Data Explorer. Call it via Scripts.ExecuteStoredProcedureAsync, supplying the partition key and an array of parameters. Stored procedures must call context.getResponse().setBody() to return a result. Key limitations: no async/await — use callbacks; execution must complete within 5 seconds; all operations must target the same partition key; no external network calls. Stored procedures are the only way to perform multi-item ACID transactions without the newer TransactionalBatch SDK API.
fj: Cosmos DB stored procedures can call external HTTP endpoints to fetch lookup data during execution.
fs: Stored procedures execute in a sandboxed JavaScript environment on Cosmos DB servers with no network access. They can only interact with documents in the same partition of the same container via the context API. External HTTP or database calls are not permitted.

code.label: Stored procedure: bulk create within a partition

q: What are Cosmos DB triggers and how do pre-triggers and post-triggers differ in behavior and use cases?
a: Cosmos DB triggers are JavaScript functions that execute before (pre-trigger) or after (post-trigger) a document operation. Pre-triggers run before the write is persisted; they can inspect and modify the incoming document (e.g., add a createdAt timestamp, validate schema). Pre-triggers throw an error to abort the operation. Post-triggers run after the write within the same transaction; they can perform additional reads or writes to other documents within the same partition. They are registered and called explicitly — triggers are not automatic; the client must specify which trigger(s) to invoke via ItemRequestOptions.PreTriggers / PostTriggers. Both types are constrained to the same partition key value. Unlike stored procedures, triggers are not directly callable on their own — they piggyback on create/replace/delete operations.
fj: Cosmos DB triggers fire automatically on every document write, similar to SQL Server triggers.
fs: Cosmos DB triggers must be explicitly requested per operation via the SDK's request options — they are not automatic. If the client does not include the trigger name in the request, it does not execute. This opt-in behavior differs fundamentally from relational DB triggers.

code.label: Invoking a pre-trigger on CreateItem

q: How do you implement and use a user-defined function (UDF) in Cosmos DB SQL?
a: User-defined functions (UDFs) are JavaScript functions registered in the container's script store that can be called from Cosmos DB SQL queries in SELECT and WHERE clauses. They are pure functions: given the same inputs they return the same output, with no side effects and no document mutations. Register via Scripts.CreateUserDefinedFunctionAsync (or portal). Call in a query using the udf. prefix: SELECT udf.computeTax(c.price) AS tax FROM c. UDFs run inline during query execution, so they incur compute cost per row evaluated. They have no access to the context object (unlike stored procedures), so they cannot read or write documents. UDFs are useful for encapsulating complex calculation logic (date formatting, business rules, unit conversions) that would be cumbersome to express in SQL alone.
fj: UDFs in Cosmos DB can call external services or read other documents to enrich query results.
fs: UDFs are pure JavaScript functions with no I/O capabilities. They receive only the arguments passed by the query, have no access to the Cosmos DB context, and cannot make network calls or read other documents. They exist solely for in-query computation.

q: Under what conditions should you add a second Azure region to a Cosmos DB account, and what does multi-region distribution guarantee?
a: Distribute data to a second region when your SLA requires 99.999% availability (versus 99.99% for single-region), when users are geographically distributed and latency to a single region is unacceptable, or when your DR policy mandates zero-RPO or fast regional failover. A multi-region Cosmos DB account replicates all data asynchronously (for most consistency levels) to every configured region. Reads can be served from the nearest region, reducing latency. Writes go to the primary write region unless multi-region write is enabled. Regional replication adds per-region storage and RU costs — charges are multiplied by the number of regions. Before distributing, evaluate: latency requirements per region, data sovereignty rules (some data cannot leave certain geographies), and the RU cost multiplier. Adding a read region does not improve write throughput.
fj: Adding a second read region doubles your total read throughput because Cosmos DB splits reads across both regions equally.
fs: Adding a read region does not increase write capacity or reduce write RU costs. It improves read latency for users near the new region and provides a failover target. Reads are routed to the preferred region, not load-balanced across all regions automatically.

q: How do you configure automatic failover policies for a Cosmos DB for NoSQL account to handle regional outages?
a: Automatic failover is enabled in the Azure portal under the Cosmos DB account's 'Replicate data globally' blade. You set a priority-ordered list of failover regions; during an outage of the write region, Cosmos DB automatically promotes the highest-priority read region to become the new write region. The failover typically completes in under 15 minutes. Enable it via the portal toggle 'Enable automatic failover', or via ARM template (enableAutomaticFailover: true) or CLI (az cosmosdb update --enable-automatic-failover true). Each region is assigned a failover priority (0 = primary write region, 1 = first failover target, etc.). You can change priorities at any time without downtime. Applications using the SDK's preferred locations list automatically discover the new write region via the account's global endpoint and the SDK's region re-routing logic.
fj: Automatic failover requires no configuration — Cosmos DB always fails over to the nearest region automatically.
fs: Automatic failover must be explicitly enabled on the account. Without it, Cosmos DB does not automatically change the write region during an outage; a manual failover by an operator is required. The failover priority order must also be configured to control which region becomes the new primary.

q: How do you perform a manual failover to move the single write region in Azure Cosmos DB?
a: Manual failover lets you move the write region to any configured read region without a real outage — useful for planned maintenance, DR drills, or region evacuation. Trigger it in the Azure portal: Cosmos DB account → Replicate data globally → Manual Failover → select the target region → confirm. Via CLI: az cosmosdb failover-priority-change --failover-policies '[{"failoverPriority":0,"locationName":"East US 2"}]'. Via PowerShell: Invoke-AzCosmosDBAccountFailover. Manual failover requires automatic failover to be disabled on the account (you cannot perform a manual failover while automatic failover is enabled, or you must temporarily disable it). The operation is fast (under a minute) but briefly makes the database read-only during the switch. Applications connected to the global endpoint automatically discover the new write region.
fj: Manual failover can be performed at any time regardless of whether automatic failover is enabled on the account.
fs: When automatic failover is enabled, Cosmos DB manages failover priority automatically and manual failover priority changes are not permitted via the same interface. You must disable automatic failover before performing a manual priority-based failover. The two modes are mutually exclusive.

q: How does the choice of consistency model in Cosmos DB affect availability and RU cost?
a: Cosmos DB offers five consistency levels from strongest to weakest: Strong, Bounded Staleness, Session, Consistent Prefix, Eventual. Strong consistency requires synchronous replication to all configured regions before a write is acknowledged, which means writes cannot proceed during a regional partition — effectively reducing availability below 99.99%. Strong also roughly doubles write RU cost because the write must be confirmed on multiple replicas before returning. Bounded Staleness requires synchronous replication to a quorum but allows a bounded lag; it provides 99.999% availability for multi-region reads. Session, Consistent Prefix, and Eventual allow asynchronous replication and do not impact write availability. For reads: Strong and Bounded Staleness in multi-region reads cost more RU than weaker levels because they may need to query the write region. Eventual reads are always served from the nearest replica at near-zero extra RU.
fj: Strong consistency has no impact on write RU cost — it only affects read latency.
fs: Strong consistency requires synchronous acknowledgment from multiple replicas before confirming a write, which approximately doubles write RU consumption compared to weaker levels. It also increases write latency by the round-trip time to the farthest replica and reduces write availability during partitions.

q: How does consistency level choice affect performance and latency in Cosmos DB multi-region deployments?
a: Strong consistency: write latency = 2× the network round-trip to the farthest replica (synchronous quorum write). Reads always go to the write region for freshness, so read latency is dominated by distance to the write region regardless of the reader's location. Bounded Staleness: write latency similar to Strong for multi-region; reads can come from the nearest region after the staleness window. Session consistency: writes are fast (acknowledged locally); reads are fast from any replica for the same session, but a different session in another region may see lag. Consistent Prefix / Eventual: lowest write and read latency — writes acknowledged after local quorum, reads served from nearest replica immediately. For a globally distributed app requiring <10 ms reads in all regions, Session or weaker is required; Strong is incompatible with that SLA in a multi-region deployment.
fj: Consistent Prefix and Eventual have identical performance characteristics in all scenarios.
fs: Consistent Prefix guarantees that reads never see out-of-order writes, while Eventual may briefly surface writes out of order. Consistent Prefix provides stronger ordering guarantees with similar latency to Eventual, but they are not identical — the ordering guarantee can prevent certain replication shortcuts.

q: How do you configure an application to read from a specific Cosmos DB replica region?
a: The SDK accepts a preferred region list (ApplicationPreferredRegions or SetCurrentLocation) that determines where reads are routed. The SDK tries regions in order and falls back to the next if the current region is unavailable. In .NET: CosmosClientOptions.ApplicationPreferredRegions = new List<string> { "West Europe", "East US" }. In Python: preferred_locations=["West Europe", "East US"]. Writes always go to the current write region regardless of this setting (unless multi-region writes are enabled). The global account endpoint (*.documents.azure.com) handles region routing automatically; you do not need region-specific endpoints. For session consistency, the SDK propagates the session token to the preferred read region automatically. Setting ApplicationPreferredRegions to the region nearest your compute resources gives the lowest read latency.
fj: To read from a specific region you must use a region-specific endpoint URL rather than the global account endpoint.
fs: Region-specific endpoints exist but are not required. The SDK's ApplicationPreferredRegions option routes reads to the preferred region through the global endpoint, which is simpler and allows automatic fallback to other regions on outage. Hard-coding a region-specific endpoint removes this resilience.

q: When should you enable multi-region writes in Cosmos DB, and what are the trade-offs?
a: Enable multi-region writes when your application requires write latency from multiple geographies simultaneously — for example, a globally distributed SaaS app where users in Asia and Europe both submit writes that must land locally within milliseconds. Multi-region writes allow any configured region to accept writes. Trade-offs: (1) Write conflicts can occur when two regions concurrently modify the same item. (2) You must configure a conflict resolution policy (Last-Write-Wins by timestamp, or a custom merge stored procedure). (3) Write RU cost increases because each write is replicated asynchronously to all other regions. (4) Strong consistency is not compatible with multi-region writes. If your write pattern is geographically concentrated (e.g., all writes from one region), single write region + multi-region reads is simpler and avoids conflict management. Multi-region writes suit active-active global patterns.
fj: Multi-region writes automatically resolve all conflicts without any application configuration.
fs: Conflicts in multi-region writes are not automatically resolved without explicit policy configuration. You must choose Last-Write-Wins (with a conflict resolution path) or a custom stored procedure. Without a policy, conflicting writes are routed to the conflict feed for manual resolution, which is not a production-ready approach.

q: How do you enable and configure multi-region writes in Azure Cosmos DB?
a: Enable multi-region writes at account creation via the portal (tick 'Multi-region writes') or post-creation via ARM template (enableMultipleWriteLocations: true), CLI (az cosmosdb update --enable-multiple-write-locations true), or PowerShell. All write regions must be pre-added to the account. The SDK must also be configured to use multi-region write mode: in .NET, CosmosClientOptions.ApplicationPreferredRegions routes writes to the nearest configured write region automatically. No special write endpoint is needed — the SDK discovers write regions via the account's metadata. After enabling, each configured region accepts writes. Note: switching from single to multi-region writes is non-destructive, but disabling multi-region writes (reverting to single write region) requires a region priority change and can cause brief write unavailability.
fj: Multi-region writes require separate write endpoint URLs for each region, which the application must route to explicitly.
fs: The Cosmos DB SDK handles write region routing automatically using ApplicationPreferredRegions or the global endpoint. The SDK selects the nearest write region and fails over to the next preferred region transparently. Application code does not need region-specific write endpoints.

q: How do you implement a custom conflict resolution policy for Azure Cosmos DB for NoSQL multi-region writes?
a: A custom conflict resolution policy uses a merge stored procedure to resolve write conflicts. The procedure receives the conflicting versions (incoming, existing, and any previous conflicts in the feed) and must decide the final state, writing it back via context. Configure in .NET: ContainerProperties.ConflictResolutionPolicy = new ConflictResolutionPolicy { Mode = ConflictResolutionMode.Custom, ConflictResolutionProcedure = $"dbs/{db}/colls/{container}/sprocs/{sprocName}" }. If the stored procedure is absent or throws, the conflict is written to the conflicts feed (/conflicts) for manual resolution. The conflicts feed is accessible via container.Conflicts and can be iterated to inspect and manually resolve outstanding conflicts. Last-Write-Wins (LWW) is simpler — it uses a numeric property path and the highest value wins — but cannot handle business-logic merge scenarios like combining two partial updates.
fj: The custom conflict resolution stored procedure runs on the client side after the SDK detects a conflict.
fs: The merge stored procedure runs server-side on Cosmos DB, automatically triggered when a conflict is detected during replication. It is not a client-side callback — the server invokes it exactly once within the replication transaction. Client code cannot intercept or modify the merge logic at resolution time.

code.label: Setting a custom conflict resolution policy

q: How do you configure Azure Cosmos DB Mirroring for Microsoft Fabric, and when should you choose it over the Spark connector?
a: Fabric Mirroring replicates Cosmos DB transactional data into Microsoft Fabric's OneLake in near-real-time using the change feed under the hood. To configure: in Microsoft Fabric, create a mirrored database, select Azure Cosmos DB as the source, provide the Cosmos DB connection string and select containers, then start replication. Fabric automatically manages the change feed consumer and lands data as Delta Parquet in OneLake. Once mirrored, query via Fabric SQL analytics endpoint or Spark. Choose Mirroring over the Spark connector when: you want a fully managed, no-code integration; you need near-real-time data in Fabric without manual pipeline maintenance; you want to query data using T-SQL via the Fabric SQL endpoint. Choose the Spark connector when: you need write-back to the transactional store from Spark; you require ad-hoc or batch reads without a persistent mirror; or you work in Azure Synapse (not Fabric) where Mirroring is not available.
fj: Fabric Mirroring replaces the analytical store in Cosmos DB and cannot coexist with it on the same container.
fs: Fabric Mirroring and the Cosmos DB analytical store are independent features that can coexist on the same account. Mirroring copies data to OneLake via the change feed, while the analytical store (Synapse Link) maintains a separate column store within Cosmos DB itself. They serve different compute surfaces (Fabric vs Synapse).

q: How do you enable the analytical store on a Cosmos DB container and what does it provide?
a: The analytical store is a fully isolated column-store copy of transactional data, updated automatically from the transactional row store via Cosmos DB Synapse Link — with no impact on provisioned RU. Enable it at container creation: in the portal, when creating a container, toggle 'Analytical store' to On. Via CLI: az cosmosdb sql container create --analytical-storage-ttl -1 (or a specific TTL in seconds). Existing containers can be migrated via the portal or CLI, but migration may take time for large containers. Once enabled, the analytical store is queryable from Azure Synapse Analytics (Spark 3 or Serverless SQL) with no ETL. It stores all document history unless bounded by an analytical TTL. The analytical store uses columnar format optimized for aggregation queries and does not charge RU for analytical reads — only for transactional reads from the operational store.
fj: Enabling the analytical store consumes additional RU from your container's provisioned throughput.
fs: The analytical store is fully decoupled from the transactional store. Synapse Link propagates changes from the transactional store to the analytical store at no RU cost to the container. Analytical queries against the analytical store do not use Cosmos DB provisioned throughput — they are billed separately as Synapse compute.

q: How do you query a Cosmos DB analytical store from Azure Synapse Spark and Azure Synapse Serverless SQL?
a: From Synapse Spark: link the Cosmos DB account in Synapse Analytics workspace via the 'Linked services' blade. Then in a notebook: spark.read.format('cosmos.olap').option('spark.synapse.linkedService', '<linked-service-name>').option('spark.cosmos.container', 'orders').load(). From Synapse Serverless SQL: use OPENROWSET with the Cosmos DB account endpoint and access key or managed identity, pointing to the analytical store endpoint. Both query paths read from the analytical store — not the transactional store — so queries do not consume Cosmos DB RU. The analytical store uses an automatically inferred schema (Full Fidelity Schema or Well-Defined Schema mode); complex nested types appear as nested columns. For Serverless SQL, CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE or CREATE VIEW over OPENROWSET is the standard pattern to expose analytical data to Power BI or other tools.
fj: Synapse Spark reads Cosmos DB data by connecting to the transactional store endpoint using the operational SDK.
fs: Synapse Spark with Synapse Link reads from the analytical store (column store), not the transactional store. Using the operational OLTP endpoint from Spark would consume provisioned RU and is the older Spark connector pattern, which Synapse Link was designed to replace for analytics.

code.label: Synapse Spark read from analytical store

q: How do you read and write to the Cosmos DB transactional store from Azure Synapse Spark using the Spark connector?
a: The Azure Cosmos DB Spark connector (azure-cosmos-spark) supports both read and write against the transactional store. Read: spark.read.format('cosmos.oltp').options({'spark.cosmos.accountEndpoint': endpoint, 'spark.cosmos.accountKey': key, 'spark.cosmos.database': db, 'spark.cosmos.container': container}).load(). Write: df.write.format('cosmos.oltp').options({...}).mode('append').save(). Writes back to the transactional store consume RU from the container's provisioned throughput, so throttling (429) is possible for large writes; use CosmosDB's bulk support options and configure spark.cosmos.write.bulk.enabled = true. Use the Spark connector for write-back scenarios (enriched results, aggregations back to Cosmos DB) or when reading a bounded dataset for ETL into another sink. Prefer the Synapse Link / analytical store path for large analytical reads to avoid RU impact.
fj: Writing data from Spark to Cosmos DB is not possible; the Spark connector supports reads only.
fs: The Cosmos DB Spark connector supports full read and write operations against the transactional store. Writes use the Cosmos DB SDK internally and consume provisioned RU. Bulk write mode (spark.cosmos.write.bulk.enabled) is strongly recommended to batch writes efficiently.

q: How do you integrate Cosmos DB change feed events with Azure Event Hubs and Azure Functions?
a: The standard integration pattern: an Azure Function with a Cosmos DB trigger listens to the change feed; inside the function, use the Event Hubs output binding to forward each batch of changed documents to an Event Hub. Alternatively, a change feed processor in a long-running app can publish messages to Event Hubs via the Event Hubs SDK. The Event Hubs trigger then fans out those events to downstream consumers. The Cosmos DB trigger uses a lease container to track change feed progress, enabling multiple Function instances to process partitions in parallel. Configure the trigger with the container name, lease container name, and the connection string. Use this pattern for event-driven architectures where Cosmos DB changes need to propagate to multiple consumers (analytics, notifications, caching) without coupling them to Cosmos DB directly.
fj: Azure Event Hubs can natively read from the Cosmos DB change feed without any Azure Functions or custom code.
fs: Event Hubs does not have a built-in Cosmos DB source connector. You need an intermediary (Azure Function with Cosmos DB trigger, or custom application) to read from the change feed and publish to Event Hubs. Azure Event Hubs is a sink, not a change feed consumer.

q: How do you use the Cosmos DB change feed with Azure Functions to denormalize data across containers?
a: Denormalization via change feed: when a source document (e.g., a Product) changes, the change feed emits an event. An Azure Function with a CosmosDBTrigger captures the change and applies the updated denormalized field (e.g., product name) to all OrderLineItem documents that embed it — using PatchItemAsync or a bulk upsert. The function must query OrderLineItems by the changed ProductId (a cross-partition query, typically on an indexed property) and update each item. This keeps denormalized copies eventually consistent without the writer needing to know about all consumers. Design considerations: the function must be idempotent (change feed guarantees at-least-once delivery); use the item's _etag or a version field to skip duplicate processing; consider partitioning updates into batches to avoid RU spikes.
fj: The change feed delivers each change exactly once, so idempotency in Azure Functions consumers is not necessary.
fs: The Cosmos DB change feed guarantees at-least-once delivery — in failure/retry scenarios a change may be delivered more than once. Functions consumers must be idempotent, checking whether the update has already been applied (e.g., via _etag or a processed-flag) before writing.

code.label: Azure Function: denormalize product name on change

q: How do you use the Cosmos DB change feed and Azure Functions to enforce referential integrity?
a: Cosmos DB does not enforce foreign-key constraints, but you can approximate referential integrity using the change feed. Pattern: when a parent document (e.g., Customer) is deleted, a delete event does not appear directly in the change feed for NoSQL (deletes require TTL-based soft-delete or a tombstone pattern). Instead: (1) mark items as deleted using a 'deleted: true' flag rather than hard-deleting; (2) the change feed emits the soft-delete update; (3) an Azure Function reads the event and cascades the deletion or archival to child documents (Orders, Addresses). For enforce-before-delete (prevent deletion of a customer with open orders), use a pre-trigger or application-layer validation before soft-deleting. The change feed approach handles post-deletion cleanup asynchronously and eventually consistent, not synchronously.
fj: The Cosmos DB change feed emits a delete event when a document is hard-deleted, enabling cascading deletes via Azure Functions.
fs: The Cosmos DB change feed does not emit events for hard-deletes. Only inserts and updates (including TTL expirations, which appear as deletes via TTL) are surfaced. To detect deletions you must use a soft-delete pattern (add a 'deleted' flag) so the change appears as an update in the feed.

q: How do you aggregate data and build reports using the Cosmos DB change feed and Azure Functions?
a: The change feed delivers a stream of document changes in insertion order per partition. An Azure Function processes each batch and maintains running aggregates in a separate Cosmos DB container (or Azure Table Storage, Redis). For example: each order insert triggers the function, which reads or creates a 'dailySummary' document for that date and increments totalRevenue and orderCount using PatchItemAsync with Increment operations. This builds a near-real-time materialized aggregate without scanning the full orders container. Cosmos DB's native aggregation queries (COUNT, SUM) are powerful but scan on demand; pre-computed aggregates via change feed are far cheaper at query time for dashboards. Ensure the aggregate update is idempotent: use the order's id as a unique event key and skip already-processed events.
fj: Running a SUM query over all orders at dashboard load time is equivalent in cost to a pre-computed aggregate built via the change feed.
fs: A query-time SUM over all orders scans every item in the container (or every item in a partition) and charges RU proportional to data volume. A change feed-maintained aggregate updates a single document incrementally; reading the aggregate is a 1 RU point read regardless of order count.

q: How do you use the Cosmos DB change feed with Azure Functions to archive data?
a: An Azure Function with a Cosmos DB trigger reads the change feed and writes documents that match archival criteria (e.g., orders older than 90 days, or items with status='closed') to a cold-storage target such as Azure Blob Storage (as JSON or Parquet), Azure Data Lake Gen2, or a cheaper Cosmos DB container with lower throughput. After successful archival, the Function can delete or TTL-expire the source documents. This pattern offloads cold data from the hot transactional container, reducing storage costs and keeping the hot container's index smaller (faster queries). The Function's lease container tracks progress so archival resumes after failures. Combine with the container's TTL: set TTL on source items after archival confirmation to let Cosmos DB clean them up automatically without extra delete operations.
fj: Azure Data Factory is the only supported tool for archiving data from Cosmos DB to Azure Blob Storage.
fs: While ADF supports Cosmos DB as a source, the change feed + Azure Functions pattern is often preferred for near-real-time, streaming archival because it avoids full scans. ADF is better suited for scheduled bulk exports, not continuous streaming archival triggered by document changes.

q: How do you implement Azure AI Search indexing for a Cosmos DB solution?
a: Azure AI Search (formerly Azure Cognitive Search) integrates with Cosmos DB via an indexer. Configure: (1) Create an AI Search data source pointing to your Cosmos DB account, database, and container, with a query filter if needed. (2) Define a search index schema matching the fields you want to make searchable. (3) Create an indexer that runs on a schedule (e.g., every 5 minutes) or on-demand; it uses the _ts (timestamp) high-water mark to detect new/modified documents since the last run. The indexer pulls documents from Cosmos DB (consuming RU) and pushes them to AI Search. For deletions, use a soft-delete column (e.g., 'isDeleted': true) with a deletion detection policy on the indexer — AI Search removes the corresponding index document when it sees the flag. This enables full-text search, faceting, and semantic ranking over Cosmos DB content without impacting transactional store query capacity.
fj: Azure AI Search reads from the Cosmos DB change feed directly, so no indexer configuration is required.
fs: Azure AI Search uses a pull-based indexer model with a high-water mark timestamp, not the change feed. It polls Cosmos DB on a schedule. The change feed is a push model consumed by the SDK or Azure Functions; the two are separate integration paths. AI Search does not natively consume the change feed.

q: How do you adjust Cosmos DB indexes to optimize query performance, and what is the default indexing policy?
a: By default, Cosmos DB automatically indexes all document properties with range indexes, enabling any property to be used in WHERE or ORDER BY clauses without configuration. However, the default policy indexes everything — including large string fields, arrays, and nested objects you may never query — consuming extra write RU and storage. Optimization steps: (1) Exclude high-write, never-queried paths (e.g., large blob strings) using 'excludedPaths'. (2) Add composite indexes for ORDER BY + filter combinations involving two or more properties. (3) Add spatial indexes only for geospatial queries. (4) Use the portal's Index Advisor (accessed via the Metrics blade) to see which indexes would reduce query RU. Every modification to the indexing policy triggers an asynchronous index transformation that runs in the background without downtime. Write RU cost is proportional to the number of indexed paths — fewer indexed paths = lower write RU.
fj: Disabling indexing on a container improves both read and write performance.
fs: Disabling indexing (setting policy mode to 'None') eliminates index write overhead but makes all queries full scans, dramatically increasing read RU cost and latency. Point reads by id + partition key still work without an index, but any filter-based query becomes extremely expensive. Selective exclusions are better than disabling entirely.

code.label: Custom indexing policy: exclude heavy paths, add composite

q: How do you calculate the RU cost of a Cosmos DB query, and what tools are available to retrieve this at runtime?
a: RU cost is returned in the 'x-ms-request-charge' HTTP response header. In the SDK: for queries, each page from ReadNextAsync() exposes a RequestCharge property; sum across all pages for the total query cost. For point operations, ItemResponse.RequestCharge gives the per-operation cost. In the Azure portal's Data Explorer, running a query shows the 'Request Charge' below the results panel. You can also retrieve diagnostics via CosmosResponseMessage.Diagnostics which includes per-partition RU breakdown. Cost influencers: number of index paths scanned, number of documents returned, document size, query complexity (JOINs, aggregates, system functions), and whether the query is single or cross-partition. Use the diagnostics to identify which partitions are most expensive and whether the query hit an index or performed a full scan.
fj: The RU cost reported by the SDK is an estimate; actual billing is calculated separately by Azure Monitor.
fs: The RequestCharge in the SDK response is the exact RU cost used for billing. It is not an estimate — Azure bills precisely on the RU consumed as reported in these response headers. Azure Monitor provides aggregated metrics over time, not per-request billing adjustments.
