q: What is the Azure Cosmos DB integrated cache and how does it reduce RU cost?
a: The integrated cache is an in-memory cache built into the Cosmos DB dedicated gateway. It caches point reads and query results. When a request hits the cache within the MaxIntegratedCacheStaleness window, Cosmos DB returns the cached response at 0 RU cost. To use it: provision a dedicated gateway (SKU: IntegratedCache), connect applications in Gateway mode pointing to the dedicated gateway endpoint. The cache is populated on the first request (cache miss charges normal RU) and subsequent identical requests within the staleness window are free. Cache staleness is controlled per-request via ItemRequestOptions.DedicatedGatewayRequestOptions.MaxIntegratedCacheStaleness (TimeSpan). The integrated cache is most beneficial for read-heavy workloads with repeated identical reads (e.g., product catalog, configuration data). It does not cache write operations. The dedicated gateway is billed hourly in addition to normal RU charges.
fj: The integrated cache works with both Gateway mode and Direct mode connections.
fs: The integrated cache is only available through Gateway mode connections pointing to the dedicated gateway endpoint. Direct mode connections bypass the gateway entirely and therefore cannot use the integrated cache. If you switch to Direct mode, all cache benefits are lost.

code.label: Reading with integrated cache staleness hint

q: How do you develop an Azure Functions trigger to process the Cosmos DB change feed?
a: The CosmosDBTrigger attribute binds the function to the change feed of a source container. Required parameters: DatabaseName, ContainerName, Connection (app setting name with the Cosmos DB connection string), and LeaseContainerName (the container that stores change feed checkpoints). Optional: CreateLeaseContainerIfNotExists (default false), StartFromBeginning (replay all history), FeedPollDelay (polling interval). The function receives IReadOnlyList<T> (or Document/JObject for schema-less) representing the batch of changed items. Azure Functions uses the change feed processor under the hood, automatically parallelizing across instances and managing leases. Deploy multiple function instances to process high-volume feeds — the lease mechanism ensures no partition is processed by two instances simultaneously. Monitor lag via the 'Estimated Lag' metric in Azure Monitor.
fj: The Cosmos DB trigger processes each changed item individually in separate function invocations.
fs: The Cosmos DB trigger batches multiple changes into a single function invocation (IReadOnlyList<T>). Processing is per-batch, not per-item. This is more efficient than per-item invocations. The batch size depends on throughput and polling interval; it is not configurable to exactly one item per invocation.

code.label: Cosmos DB triggered Azure Function

q: How do you consume the Cosmos DB change feed from within an application using the SDK change feed processor?
a: The change feed processor (part of Cosmos DB SDK v3+) uses four components: monitored container (source), lease container (checkpoint store), compute instance name (for multi-instance scale-out identification), and a delegate (IChangeFeedObserver or handler). Build it via ChangeFeedProcessorBuilder: container.GetChangeFeedProcessorBuilder<T>(processorName, handleChanges).WithInstanceName(instanceName).WithLeaseContainer(leaseContainer).Build(). Call StartAsync() to begin. The handler receives batches of changed documents as IReadOnlyCollection<T> along with a CancellationToken. Stop cleanly with StopAsync(). The processor automatically checkpoints after each successful batch. Multiple instances can run simultaneously with the same processorName — the lease container coordinates partition assignments. Use this pattern when you need change feed processing in a long-running service (e.g., .NET worker service, Kubernetes pod) rather than Azure Functions.
fj: The change feed processor pulls from the change feed using SQL queries with an _ts filter on a schedule.
fs: The change feed processor uses Cosmos DB's native change feed API, not SQL queries with timestamp filters. The change feed API maintains server-side continuation tokens per partition, ensuring no changes are missed and enabling efficient resumption after restarts — something polling-by-timestamp cannot guarantee for high-frequency workloads.

code.label: Change feed processor setup in .NET

q: How do you use the change feed estimator to monitor the number of change feed instances and processing lag?
a: The change feed estimator queries the lease container to calculate the estimated number of remaining changes (lag) per partition. Build it via: monitoredContainer.GetChangeFeedEstimator(processorName, leaseContainer). Call GetCurrentStateAsync() to get an IAsyncEnumerable<ChangeFeedProcessorState> with per-partition lag. Aggregate the estimated lag values to get total unprocessed changes. Use this in a monitoring loop or Azure Monitor custom metric to detect backlogs. If lag is growing, add more processor instances (same processorName, different instanceName) — the lease container automatically redistributes partition assignments. If too many instances exist for the number of physical partitions, excess instances are idle (each physical partition can have at most one active processor). The estimator itself does not consume RU proportional to data size — it only reads the lease documents.
fj: Adding more change feed processor instances always reduces lag because each instance processes more changes per second.
fs: The number of active processor instances is bounded by the number of physical partitions — each physical partition can be assigned to at most one processor instance at a time. Adding more instances beyond the partition count leaves extras idle. Scale the container's throughput (and therefore physical partitions) to increase parallelism beyond this ceiling.

q: When should you choose a read-heavy versus write-heavy index strategy in Cosmos DB?
a: A read-heavy index strategy maximizes indexed paths: include all paths used in queries, add composite indexes for ORDER BY / multi-property filters, and accept higher per-write RU cost. Best when: the workload is OLTP with many ad-hoc query patterns, writes are infrequent or moderate, and query latency is more critical than write throughput. A write-heavy strategy minimizes indexed paths: use excludedPaths to exclude fields never queried, set indexing mode to 'lazy' (asynchronous indexing — lower write latency but queries may not see very recent writes) or even 'none' for write-optimized staging containers. Best when: bulk-loading data, appending time-series data with a known partition key query, or using the container purely as a write buffer before ETL. Lazy indexing risks returning slightly stale query results but dramatically reduces write RU.
fj: Lazy indexing mode guarantees that all documents are indexed before any query can be issued.
fs: Lazy indexing mode indexes documents asynchronously in the background; there is a window during which newly written documents are not yet indexed. Queries issued immediately after a write may not return those documents. For workloads requiring read-after-write consistency on queries, use consistent indexing mode.

q: What are the three Cosmos DB index types (range, spatial, composite) and when do you use each?
a: Range indexes are the default and support equality, range comparison (>, <, >=, <=), ORDER BY, and string comparisons on scalar properties. They are applied to all paths by default and cover the majority of query patterns. Spatial indexes are required for geospatial queries using ST_DISTANCE, ST_WITHIN, ST_INTERSECTS on GeoJSON Point, Polygon, LineString, or MultiPolygon properties. Without an explicit spatial index definition, geospatial queries fail or return errors. Composite indexes are required for multi-property ORDER BY (e.g., ORDER BY c.lastName ASC, c.firstName ASC) and improve performance of queries with multiple filter + sort combinations. A composite index must list properties in the exact order and direction matching the query. Composite indexes also reduce RU cost for queries combining equality filters and range sorts on different properties.
fj: Range indexes support geospatial ST_DISTANCE queries without any additional spatial index configuration.
fs: Range indexes do not support geospatial functions. ST_DISTANCE, ST_WITHIN, and ST_INTERSECTS require an explicit spatial index on the GeoJSON property path. Without a spatial index, Cosmos DB returns an error for geospatial queries.

q: How do you configure a custom indexing policy via the Azure portal, and what is the JSON structure?
a: In the Azure portal: Data Explorer → select container → Settings → Indexing Policy. The policy is a JSON document with these fields: 'indexingMode' ('consistent', 'lazy', or 'none'); 'includedPaths' (array of path objects with 'path' and optional 'indexes' array specifying type and precision); 'excludedPaths' (paths to omit from indexing); 'compositeIndexes' (array of arrays, each sub-array defining a multi-property composite); 'spatialIndexes' (array of path + types). Click Save to apply; a background transformation starts and progress is visible via the SDK (container.ReadAsync().IndexTransformationProgress). The portal provides a visual editor and raw JSON toggle. Changes to excluded paths reduce write RU immediately after transformation completes.
fj: Changing the indexing policy on an existing container causes a brief downtime while Cosmos DB rebuilds the index.
fs: Indexing policy changes on existing containers trigger a background transformation that runs asynchronously without any downtime. The container remains fully readable and writable during transformation. Existing queries continue to work using the old index until transformation completes.

q: How do you implement a composite index in Cosmos DB and what query patterns require one?
a: A composite index is required for: (1) ORDER BY on multiple properties (e.g., ORDER BY c.lastName ASC, c.age DESC); (2) queries combining a range filter on one property with ORDER BY on another (e.g., WHERE c.age > 30 ORDER BY c.name); (3) can improve efficiency of equality + range filter combinations. Define it in the indexing policy under 'compositeIndexes' — each composite is an ordered array of {path, order} objects. The order and direction must match the query exactly; a composite index defined as [lastName ASC, firstName ASC] will NOT be used for ORDER BY lastName DESC, firstName DESC. Add composite indexes only for query patterns you know exist — unused composite indexes add write RU overhead with no benefit.
fj: A composite index allows ORDER BY on any two properties regardless of their order in the composite definition.
fs: Composite index order and direction must match the query's ORDER BY clause exactly, or reversed exactly (all ASC flipped to DESC). A partial match (e.g., first property matches but second does not) means the composite index is not used for that query, and the query falls back to a full scan or cross-partition sort.

code.label: Composite index for multi-property ORDER BY

q: What techniques do you apply to optimize index performance in a Cosmos DB container?
a: Key optimization techniques: (1) Exclude high-cardinality string or binary fields never used in filters (e.g., base64 images, raw JSON blobs) via excludedPaths. (2) Add composite indexes for known multi-property ORDER BY or filter+sort patterns. (3) Use lazy indexing for write-optimized containers where queries can tolerate slight staleness. (4) Monitor 'IndexUtilization' in query diagnostics (QueryMetrics.IndexUtilizationInfo) to see which indexed paths were used and whether the query hit an index or performed a scan. (5) Use the portal's Index Metrics feature (preview) to get recommendations. (6) Remove unused composite or spatial indexes that add write RU without serving any query. (7) For analytical workloads with no transactional queries, set indexing mode to 'none' and query via the analytical store instead.
fj: Adding more indexes always improves overall Cosmos DB container performance.
fs: Every additional index path adds overhead to every write operation proportional to the number of indexed paths. Over-indexing increases write RU cost, latency, and storage. Optimal index performance requires matching indexes precisely to actual query patterns — neither too few nor too many.

q: How do you evaluate HTTP response status codes and failure metrics in Azure Cosmos DB to diagnose operational issues?
a: Key Cosmos DB status codes: 200 OK (success), 201 Created, 204 No Content (delete success), 400 Bad Request (malformed query or invalid document), 401 Unauthorized (bad key or RBAC mismatch), 403 Forbidden (throughput limit exceeded on serverless, or RBAC insufficient), 404 Not Found (item does not exist), 409 Conflict (id collision), 412 Precondition Failed (ETag mismatch), 413 Request Entity Too Large (item >2 MB), 429 Too Many Requests (RU throttled), 503 Service Unavailable (transient). In Azure Monitor, the 'Total Requests' and 'Throttled Requests' metrics broken down by StatusCode show failure rates. 429s indicate under-provisioning or hot partitions. 503s indicate transient connectivity; the SDK retries automatically. Use Log Analytics with Diagnostic Logs enabled to query request-level detail including partition key range and RU consumed.
fj: A 403 response from Cosmos DB always means the account key is wrong.
fs: 403 can occur for multiple reasons beyond a wrong key: RBAC permissions insufficient for the requested operation, IP firewall blocking the client, or serverless throughput limits exceeded. Diagnose by checking the error message body for context before assuming a key issue.

q: How do you monitor the Normalized RU Consumption metric in Azure Monitor for a Cosmos DB account?
a: Normalized RU Consumption is a percentage metric (0–100%) that shows the maximum RU utilization across all physical partitions in an interval relative to the container's provisioned throughput. A value of 100% means at least one physical partition was fully saturated and throttling occurred. Navigate to the Cosmos DB account → Metrics → select 'Normalized RU Consumption'. Apply a split by 'PhysicalPartitionId' to identify which partition is hot. Sustained values above 70–80% suggest you need to increase throughput or re-evaluate partition key distribution. Set an Azure Monitor alert rule on this metric with threshold 80% to get proactive warnings before throttling impacts latency. Unlike raw RU consumption metrics, Normalized RU Consumption is already normalized to provisioned throughput, making it meaningful regardless of absolute RU values.
fj: Normalized RU Consumption above 50% always indicates a partition key design problem that requires re-partitioning.
fs: High Normalized RU Consumption can result from a hot partition (design problem) or simply insufficient provisioned throughput for a uniformly busy workload. Check the per-partition split first: if one partition is disproportionately high, it's a hot partition; if all partitions are uniformly high, increase provisioned RU.

q: How do you monitor server-side latency in Cosmos DB using Azure Monitor?
a: Azure Monitor exposes the 'Server Side Latency' metric for Cosmos DB, measured in milliseconds as the time Cosmos DB spends processing a request server-side (excluding network round-trip). Navigate to Cosmos DB account → Metrics → 'Server Side Latency'. Filter or split by OperationType (read, write, query), CollectionName, and Region. P50, P95, and P99 percentiles are available. High server-side latency despite low RU consumption suggests: complex queries requiring full scans, large document sizes, or overloaded partitions. High server-side latency with high RU often means heavy cross-partition queries or missing composite indexes. Correlate server-side latency with client-observed latency to identify whether the overhead is on the server (Cosmos DB) or the network. Cosmos DB SLA for point reads is <10 ms at P99 server-side for single region.
fj: Server-side latency in Azure Monitor includes the network round-trip time from the client to Cosmos DB.
fs: Server-side latency measures only the time Cosmos DB spends processing the request on its servers. Network latency (client to Cosmos DB) is not included. Total client-observed latency = server-side latency + network round-trip. To diagnose network issues, compare client SDK latency against server-side metrics.

q: How do you monitor data replication latency and availability in a multi-region Cosmos DB account?
a: Azure Monitor provides 'Replication Latency' metric (P99, P50) under the Cosmos DB account, which shows the lag between a write being committed in the write region and it being readable in each read region. Navigate to Metrics → 'Replication Latency' → split by 'TargetRegion'. High replication latency (>15 seconds) may indicate network congestion between regions or Cosmos DB service issues. For availability monitoring, 'Service Availability' metric reports the percentage of successful non-429 requests. Combine replication latency alerts with Azure Monitor alerts to receive notifications when replication exceeds an SLA threshold. For Bounded Staleness consistency, the configured maximum staleness (time or operations) defines the acceptable lag; monitor replication latency to ensure the actual lag stays within the configured bound.
fj: Replication latency in Cosmos DB is always zero for Session consistency because reads can come from any region.
fs: Session consistency does not eliminate replication latency — it guarantees that a client reads its own writes within the same session, but other sessions in other regions may read data that is behind the write region. Replication latency is a physical characteristic of the network and replication pipeline, independent of consistency level.

q: How do you configure Azure Monitor alerts for Azure Cosmos DB to detect performance and availability issues?
a: In the Azure portal: Cosmos DB account → Alerts → Create alert rule. Select the signal: metric (e.g., 'Normalized RU Consumption'), Activity Log event (e.g., 'Delete DatabaseAccount'), or Log query from Diagnostic Logs. Configure the condition threshold (e.g., Normalized RU > 80% averaged over 5 minutes), aggregation type (Average, Maximum), and evaluation frequency (1–5 minutes). Add an Action Group that sends email, SMS, webhook (for PagerDuty/OpsGenie), or triggers an Azure Function. Common alert rules: (1) Normalized RU > 80% for throughput saturation. (2) Total Requests with StatusCode=429 > 0 for throttling. (3) Server Side Latency P99 > 10 ms for SLA breach. (4) Service Availability < 99.99% for outage detection. (5) Replication Latency > 30s for geo-replication lag. Alerts can also be defined in ARM templates or Bicep for IaC deployments.
fj: Azure Monitor alerts for Cosmos DB can only be set on metric signals, not on diagnostic log queries.
fs: Azure Monitor supports alert rules on three signal types for Cosmos DB: Metrics (real-time numeric data), Activity Logs (control-plane events like configuration changes), and Log Queries (KQL queries against Diagnostic Logs in a Log Analytics workspace). Log query alerts are especially powerful for detecting specific error patterns in request-level diagnostic data.

q: How do you enable and query Azure Monitor resource logs (Diagnostic Logs) for Azure Cosmos DB using KQL?
a: Enable Diagnostic Logs: Cosmos DB account → Diagnostic settings → Add diagnostic setting. Select log categories: DataPlaneRequests (all CRUD/query operations), QueryRuntimeStatistics (per-query metrics including RU and partition hits), PartitionKeyStatistics, ControlPlaneRequests. Send to a Log Analytics workspace. Logs appear in the AzureDiagnostics or resource-specific tables (CDBDataPlaneRequests, CDBQueryRuntimeStatistics). Sample KQL: query for 429s by partition over the last hour. The QueryRuntimeStatistics category is especially useful for identifying expensive queries — it logs RU cost, query text, and partition key range per execution. Use these logs to correlate client errors with server-side events, audit data access patterns, and fulfill compliance requirements.
fj: Diagnostic Logs for Cosmos DB are enabled by default on all new accounts.
fs: Diagnostic Logs must be explicitly enabled via Diagnostic Settings on each Cosmos DB account. They are not enabled by default. Without enabling them, no request-level telemetry is captured in Log Analytics, and you only have aggregated metric data from Azure Monitor.

code.label: KQL: find top 5 throttled partitions in last hour

q: How do you monitor throughput and data distribution across physical partitions in Cosmos DB?
a: Use the Azure portal's Insights workbook (Cosmos DB account → Insights → Storage) to see per-partition data distribution and hot-partition indicators. Azure Monitor Metrics: 'Normalized RU Consumption' split by PhysicalPartitionId shows throughput distribution; large variance between partitions indicates a hot partition. 'Physical Partition Size' metric shows data distribution — any partition approaching 50 GB (soft warning, hard limit is higher but logical partition limit is 20 GB) requires partition key adjustment. The SDK's FeedResponse from container.GetPartitionKeyRangesAsync() returns the physical partition ranges, which combined with per-partition RU metrics gives a full picture. For key-level analysis, PartitionKeyStatistics diagnostic log category records estimated storage per partition key value — useful for identifying which key values are growing disproportionately.
fj: Azure Cosmos DB automatically rebalances data across physical partitions to maintain even distribution at all times.
fs: Cosmos DB does physically split partitions when they approach their storage limits, but this does not correct a hot-key problem. If a single logical partition key value receives disproportionate traffic, physical partition splits do not help because all requests for that key value still target the same logical partition. Hot partitions require a partition key redesign.

q: How do you monitor security events in Cosmos DB using logging and auditing?
a: Enable the ControlPlaneRequests diagnostic log category to capture all management operations (account creation, key regeneration, region changes, firewall modifications) — these are critical for security audits. Enable DataPlaneRequests to log all data-plane access with the caller identity (when using Entra ID / RBAC, the principalId is logged; with key-based auth, the key type is logged). Send logs to a Log Analytics workspace and use KQL to alert on suspicious patterns: access from unexpected IP ranges, key regeneration events, bulk deletes, or access by unexpected service principals. Azure Defender for Cosmos DB (part of Microsoft Defender for Cloud) provides threat detection for anomalous access patterns (SQL injection-like queries, unusual data exfiltration volumes, access from Tor exit nodes). Enable it from the Defender for Cloud settings on the subscription.
fj: Cosmos DB automatically sends all audit logs to Azure Security Center without any configuration.
fs: Cosmos DB diagnostic logs and Azure Defender for Cosmos DB are both opt-in features requiring explicit configuration. Audit logs must be routed to Log Analytics or Storage via Diagnostic Settings. Defender for Cosmos DB must be enabled per-account or at the subscription level in Defender for Cloud settings.

q: How do periodic and continuous backup modes in Cosmos DB differ, and how do you choose between them?
a: Periodic backup (default for existing accounts): Cosmos DB takes full backups every 1–24 hours (configurable) and stores 2 copies in a paired region. Retention is 2–720 hours. Restore requires opening a Microsoft support ticket — you cannot self-serve restore. Continuous backup: captures every change in near-real-time using the change feed. Supports self-serve point-in-time restore to any second within the retention window (7 days free tier, 30 days paid). Restore creates a new account. Continuous backup costs more (storage charged per GB of backup). Choose periodic when: low RTO/RPO is not required, cost sensitivity is high, and support-ticket restore is acceptable. Choose continuous when: regulatory requirements mandate point-in-time recovery, RPO < 1 minute is needed, or self-serve restore without a support ticket is important. Migration from periodic to continuous is one-way and irreversible.
fj: With periodic backup you can restore to any point in time within the backup retention window via the Azure portal.
fs: Periodic backup is interval-based, not continuous. You can only restore to the exact backup snapshot times (every N hours), not to arbitrary points in time. Only continuous backup mode supports true point-in-time restore to any second within the retention window.

q: How do you configure periodic backup on a Cosmos DB account and what are the available settings?
a: Periodic backup is the default mode for new accounts. Configure via: Azure portal → Cosmos DB account → Backup & Restore → Policy: Periodic. Configurable settings: Backup Interval (minimum 1 hour, maximum 24 hours; default 4 hours), Backup Retention (minimum 8 hours, maximum 720 hours / 30 days; default 8 hours), Backup Storage Redundancy (Local, Zone, Geo — default Geo replicates backups to a paired region). The backups are managed by Cosmos DB and are not directly accessible in your storage account. To restore, open an Azure support ticket — provide the account name, database, container, approximate restore point, and destination account name. Cosmos DB restores to a new account (not in-place). Periodic backup does not incur additional charges for the default settings, but longer retention and zone/local redundancy may affect cost.
fj: You can access periodic backup files directly in your Azure Storage account to restore selectively.
fs: Periodic backup files are stored in Microsoft-managed storage, not in your own Storage account. They are not directly accessible. You must request a restore through Azure Support, which creates a new Cosmos DB account populated with the restored data.

q: How do you configure continuous backup and perform a point-in-time restore on a Cosmos DB account?
a: Enable continuous backup at account creation (portal: select 'Continuous' under Backup Policy) or migrate from periodic via portal/CLI/PowerShell (one-way migration). Two tiers: Continuous 7-day (free backup storage) and Continuous 30-day (paid). To restore: Azure portal → Cosmos DB account → Backup & Restore → Restore. Select: restore point (any timestamp within retention window), target region, target account name, and which databases/containers to restore. The restore creates a new Cosmos DB account. Via CLI: az cosmosdb restore --target-database-account-name <new-name> --restore-timestamp <ISO8601> --location <region> --account-name <source>. Via PowerShell: Restore-AzCosmosDBAccount. The restore is a background operation; monitor progress via the source account's 'Restore' blade or az cosmosdb show.
fj: Continuous backup restore overwrites the existing Cosmos DB account in-place with the selected point-in-time state.
fs: Continuous backup restore always creates a new, separate Cosmos DB account. The original account is untouched. This design prevents accidental data loss during restore and allows you to verify the restored data before decommissioning the source account.

q: How do you locate the appropriate restore point for a Cosmos DB point-in-time restore after accidental data deletion?
a: The goal is to identify the timestamp just before the accidental event. Steps: (1) Check the DataPlaneRequests diagnostic logs in Log Analytics for the delete operation — filter by OperationType='delete' and ItemId of the affected documents to find the exact timestamp. (2) If Diagnostic Logs are not enabled, check Azure Activity Log for container-level delete operations. (3) Use the continuous backup Restorable APIs to list restorable databases and containers (az cosmosdb sql restorable-container list) and find the container creation/deletion events with timestamps. (4) Choose a restore point 1–2 minutes before the identified deletion timestamp to ensure the deleted items are present in the restore. The earliest restorable time is visible in the portal's Restore blade as the 'Earliest Restore Time'.
fj: You must restore to exactly the last backup snapshot timestamp — point-in-time selection is only available in 1-hour increments.
fs: With continuous backup, restore timestamps are precise to the second — not limited to hourly snapshots. You can restore to any second within the retention window. The 1-hour granularity limitation applies only to periodic backup mode.

q: How do you restore a specific database or container from a Cosmos DB continuous backup restore point?
a: Granular restore (restoring a specific database or container rather than the full account) is supported in continuous backup mode. Via portal: Restore blade → select 'Custom' restore scope → choose specific databases and containers. Via CLI: az cosmosdb restore with --databases-to-restore parameter specifying database name and optional container names. Via PowerShell: Restore-AzCosmosDBAccount with -DatabasesToRestore parameter. The restore targets a new account. If restoring only specific containers, the other containers are not included in the new account. After restore, validate the data in the new account, then if satisfied, either redirect application traffic to the new account or export/import the specific documents back to the original account using the Cosmos DB Spark connector or Data Factory.
fj: Restoring a container from continuous backup automatically redirects application connections to the restored data.
fs: Restore creates a new Cosmos DB account; no traffic is automatically redirected. You must manually update application connection strings or use a DNS/Azure Front Door abstraction layer to switch traffic. The original (corrupted/deleted) account remains unchanged until explicitly decommissioned.

q: How do you choose between service-managed and customer-managed encryption keys for Cosmos DB, and what does each involve operationally?
a: Cosmos DB encrypts all data at rest by default using service-managed keys (SMK) — Microsoft manages the key lifecycle with no action required from the customer. Customer-managed keys (CMK) add a second layer of encryption where the customer stores the encryption key in Azure Key Vault (or Azure Key Vault Managed HSM). CMK is required for: regulatory compliance mandates (HIPAA, FedRAMP) requiring customer control of encryption keys, the ability to revoke access to data by deleting the key, and audit trails of key usage. CMK trade-offs: higher operational complexity (Key Vault must be available for all Cosmos DB operations — if Key Vault is unavailable or the key is deleted, the Cosmos DB account becomes inaccessible), key rotation management, and additional Key Vault cost. Choose SMK unless compliance or security policy explicitly requires CMK.
fj: Customer-managed keys provide better read and write performance than service-managed keys.
fs: CMK adds a Key Vault call to the cryptographic path, which introduces slight latency overhead compared to SMK. CMK is chosen for compliance and key control reasons, not performance improvement.

q: How do you configure network-level access control for Azure Cosmos DB?
a: Three network access control mechanisms: (1) IP Firewall: allowlist specific IP addresses or CIDR ranges (portal → Cosmos DB account → Networking → Firewall and virtual networks). Add your client IPs, Azure Portal IP ranges, and Azure service IPs. (2) Virtual Network Service Endpoints: configure Cosmos DB to accept traffic only from specific Azure VNet subnets (without private endpoint). Traffic still traverses the Microsoft backbone but is restricted to specified subnets. (3) Private Endpoint: creates a private IP in your VNet for Cosmos DB; all traffic stays within the Azure private network. Disable public network access entirely when using private endpoints for maximum network isolation (portal → Networking → Public network access: Disabled). For Azure PaaS services (Functions, App Service), use service endpoints or private endpoints with VNet integration to restrict access without managing IP lists.
fj: IP firewall rules and virtual network rules can be applied simultaneously, but private endpoints replace both.
fs: IP firewall, VNet service endpoints, and private endpoints are complementary, not mutually exclusive. You can use IP rules alongside VNet rules for hybrid scenarios. Private endpoints provide the strongest isolation but do not automatically remove IP firewall rules — you must explicitly disable public access when using private endpoints to prevent IP-based access.

q: How does Azure Cosmos DB encrypt data, and what encryption options are available?
a: Cosmos DB encrypts all data at rest using AES-256 by default (service-managed keys) — both transactional and backup data. Data in transit is encrypted via TLS 1.2+. The analytical store (Synapse Link) also encrypts stored data. For field-level encryption, Cosmos DB supports Always Encrypted (client-side encryption) using the Cosmos DB SDK with the Always Encrypted feature, where specific document fields are encrypted on the client before sending to Cosmos DB — Cosmos DB never sees plaintext for those fields. Customer-managed keys (CMK) via Azure Key Vault add a second envelope encryption layer over the service-managed keys. All encryption is transparent to application code for at-rest and in-transit encryption; Always Encrypted requires SDK configuration and key management. There is no option to disable at-rest encryption.
fj: You can disable TLS encryption for Cosmos DB connections to improve performance in trusted networks.
fs: TLS encryption for data in transit to Cosmos DB is mandatory and cannot be disabled. All connections must use TLS 1.2 or higher. There is no performance benefit from disabling TLS — the overhead is negligible compared to network latency.

q: How do you manage control plane access to Cosmos DB using Azure RBAC?
a: Control plane operations (creating/deleting accounts, databases, containers; managing throughput; changing settings) are managed via Azure RBAC at the subscription/resource group/account scope. Built-in roles: Cosmos DB Account Reader (read-only control plane), Cosmos DB Operator (manage accounts but not read keys), DocumentDB Account Contributor (full control plane access). Assign roles via portal → Cosmos DB account → Access Control (IAM) → Add role assignment, or via CLI: az role assignment create. Use least-privilege: application service principals that only need to run queries should not have Cosmos DB Operator or Contributor roles. For infrastructure pipelines (ARM/Bicep deployments), grant Contributor at the resource group scope. Control plane RBAC does not grant data-plane access (creating/reading documents) — that requires separate data-plane RBAC or key-based auth.
fj: Assigning the Cosmos DB Operator role to a service principal grants it the ability to read and write documents.
fs: Cosmos DB Operator is a control plane role that allows management of the Cosmos DB account itself (throughput, regions, settings) but does not grant data-plane access. Document read/write requires data-plane RBAC roles (Cosmos DB Built-in Data Reader/Contributor) or account keys.

q: How do you manage data plane access to Cosmos DB using Microsoft Entra ID?
a: Cosmos DB for NoSQL supports data-plane RBAC via Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure AD). Built-in roles: 'Cosmos DB Built-in Data Reader' (read documents and containers), 'Cosmos DB Built-in Data Contributor' (read, write, delete documents and containers). Assign via CLI: az cosmosdb sql role assignment create --account-name --resource-group --role-definition-id --principal-id --scope. Or portal: Cosmos DB account → Data Explorer → Settings → Role assignments. The scope can be the full account, a specific database, or a specific container. To use RBAC, configure the SDK with a TokenCredential (DefaultAzureCredential, ManagedIdentityCredential) instead of an account key. Optionally, disable key-based access entirely with az cosmosdb update --disable-local-auth true — forcing all access through Entra ID. This eliminates the risk of leaked account keys.
fj: To use Microsoft Entra ID for Cosmos DB data access, you must also grant Azure RBAC control plane roles to the identity.
fs: Data plane RBAC and control plane RBAC are independent. A service principal can have only Cosmos DB Built-in Data Contributor (data plane) without any control plane Azure RBAC role, and it can read and write documents without being able to modify account settings. The two permission planes are separate.

code.label: Connect using DefaultAzureCredential (no keys)
lines: // Requires Cosmos DB Built-in Data Contributor role on the identity

q: How do you configure CORS settings for a Cosmos DB account to allow browser-based access?
a: CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) settings allow web browsers to make direct JavaScript SDK calls to Cosmos DB from a different origin domain. Configure via portal: Cosmos DB account → CORS → Add allowed origin (e.g., https://myapp.azurewebsites.net). Multiple origins can be listed; wildcard (*) permits all origins (acceptable for public read-only APIs, risky for write access). Via CLI: az cosmosdb update --cors '[{"allowedOrigins":"https://myapp.azurewebsites.net"}]'. CORS applies to the Cosmos DB JS SDK used in browser applications. For server-side SDKs (.NET, Python, Java), CORS is irrelevant — it only applies to browser-to-server requests. Misconfigured CORS (overly broad or missing headers) causes 'CORS error' failures in browser console even when server-side connectivity is fine.
fj: CORS settings on Cosmos DB must be configured to allow server-side .NET SDK connections from Azure App Service.
fs: CORS is a browser security mechanism for controlling which origins can make cross-domain HTTP requests from JavaScript running in a browser. Server-side SDKs (.NET, Java, Python) running in Azure App Service, Functions, or VMs are not subject to CORS restrictions — they connect directly via TCP/TLS and do not send CORS preflight requests.

q: How do you manage Cosmos DB account keys using Azure Key Vault to avoid hardcoded credentials?
a: Store Cosmos DB connection strings or account keys in Azure Key Vault as secrets. Applications retrieve them at startup using the Azure Key Vault SDK or managed identity references. In Azure App Service / Functions: use Key Vault references in application settings (syntax: @Microsoft.KeyVault(SecretUri=https://myvault.vault.azure.net/secrets/CosmosKey/)). The App Service's managed identity must have Key Vault Secrets User role. Cosmos DB supports rotating keys (primary and secondary) without downtime — generate a new key for the secondary slot, update Key Vault with the new value, let applications pick it up, then regenerate the primary key. Key Vault's soft-delete and purge protection prevent accidental key deletion. Regularly rotate keys (quarterly or per-policy) to limit the exposure window of leaked keys. Prefer Entra ID RBAC over key-based auth to eliminate key management entirely.
fj: Rotating a Cosmos DB account key immediately invalidates all active SDK connections using that key.
fs: Cosmos DB provides two keys (primary and secondary) for zero-downtime rotation. The rotation process: update connections to use the secondary key, regenerate the primary key (which invalidates only the primary), then update connections back to the new primary. No active connection is interrupted during a properly executed rotation.

q: How do you implement customer-managed keys (CMK) for Cosmos DB encryption, and what are the requirements?
a: Requirements: an Azure Key Vault with soft-delete and purge protection enabled in the same Azure region as the Cosmos DB account (recommended) or a paired region. The key must be RSA (2048 or 3072 or 4096 bit) or RSA-HSM. Steps: (1) Create the Key Vault and key. (2) Grant the Cosmos DB service principal (or system-assigned managed identity) Key Vault Crypto Service Encryption User role (get, wrap key, unwrap key). (3) When creating the Cosmos DB account: specify the key URI in 'Customer-managed key' field (portal) or keyVaultKeyUri parameter (ARM/CLI). (4) CMK is set at account creation — existing accounts without CMK cannot be migrated to CMK in place. The account is encrypted with an envelope: a Data Encryption Key (DEK) is wrapped with the CMK. If the CMK is deleted or access is revoked, the account becomes inaccessible.
fj: You can enable CMK on an existing Cosmos DB account that currently uses service-managed keys at any time.
fs: CMK must be configured at Cosmos DB account creation. Migrating an existing SMK-encrypted account to CMK is not supported — you would need to create a new CMK-enabled account and migrate data to it. Plan CMK requirements before provisioning the account.

q: How does Always Encrypted work in Cosmos DB and when should you use it?
a: Always Encrypted (AE) in Cosmos DB is client-side field-level encryption. The application uses the Cosmos DB SDK with an encryption library that encrypts specified document fields before sending to Cosmos DB and decrypts them after retrieval — Cosmos DB never sees plaintext for those fields. Key hierarchy: a Column Encryption Key (CEK) encrypts/decrypts field values; a Column Master Key (CMK, stored in Azure Key Vault) wraps the CEK. Configure via EncryptionKeyWrapMetadata and setting CosmosDataEncryptionKey on the container's ClientEncryptionPolicy. Two encryption types: Deterministic (same plaintext → same ciphertext, supports equality queries on encrypted fields) and Randomized (different ciphertext each time, more secure, but encrypted fields are not queryable). Use AE when: specific fields contain PII, PHI, or secrets that must never be visible to the database service, meeting compliance requirements like GDPR field-level protection. AE is incompatible with Synapse Link analytical store queries on encrypted fields.
fj: Always Encrypted fields can be used in range queries (>, <) when configured with Deterministic encryption.
fs: Deterministic encryption supports only equality queries (=) on encrypted fields — the ciphertext is consistent for the same plaintext, enabling equality comparisons. Range queries (>, <, BETWEEN) are not supported because ciphertext order does not correspond to plaintext order. Randomized encryption supports no query filtering at all.

q: How do you choose a data movement strategy for Azure Cosmos DB migrations and bulk operations?
a: Decision factors: source system, data volume, latency requirements, transformation needs, and whether real-time or batch is acceptable. Options: (1) Client SDK Bulk Support — best for programmatic migration from another Cosmos DB account or custom source with complex transformation logic; handles up to tens of millions of items with proper parallelism. (2) Azure Data Factory (ADF) / Synapse Pipelines — best for structured migrations from SQL databases, Storage, or other cloud systems; visual pipeline, built-in Cosmos DB connector, scalable with DIUs; no code required. (3) Azure Stream Analytics — best for streaming data from IoT Hub or Event Hubs into Cosmos DB in real time. (4) Spark Connector — best for bulk migration from Spark-accessible sources (Data Lake, Databricks, HDInsight). (5) Kafka Connector — best for streaming from Kafka topics. (6) Azure CLI / PowerShell bulk scripts — for small-scale or control-plane migrations. The Cosmos DB Data Migration Tool (open source) is another option for JSON/CSV/SQL imports.
fj: Azure Data Factory is the best tool for all Cosmos DB data movement scenarios regardless of source system.
fs: ADF excels at batch, scheduled, structured data migrations between supported connectors. For real-time streaming (IoT Hub, Kafka), Stream Analytics or the Kafka connector is more appropriate. For complex programmatic transformations or existing Spark infrastructure, the Spark connector is better. Tool selection depends on source system and latency requirements.

q: How do you move data into or out of Cosmos DB using client SDK bulk operations?
a: Enable AllowBulkExecution = true on CosmosClientOptions (.NET) or the equivalent in other SDKs. For migration: read from the source system, shape data into Cosmos DB documents (add id and partition key), fire concurrent UpsertItemAsync calls without awaiting each individually, and collect all tasks in a list. Await Task.WhenAll at the end of each batch (e.g., 1000-item batches). Monitor RU consumption and implement back-pressure if 429s are received (catch CosmosException with StatusCode 429 and delay). For exporting: iterate the container with GetItemQueryIterator, write each batch to the target system. Bulk operations consume significant RU; provision autoscale or temporarily increase throughput during the migration window. Disable non-essential indexing during bulk load (set policy to lazy or none) to reduce write RU, then re-enable and wait for index transformation.
fj: SDK bulk operations for Cosmos DB do not require increased throughput because the SDK optimizes RU usage automatically.
fs: SDK bulk support optimizes batching and parallelism but does not reduce the RU cost per item. Each item still consumes RU proportional to its size and operation type. Bulk load of millions of items will throttle on default 400 RU/s; provisioning adequate throughput for the migration window is essential.

q: How do you move data using Azure Data Factory or Azure Synapse pipelines with Cosmos DB as source or sink?
a: ADF / Synapse pipelines support Cosmos DB for NoSQL as both a source and a sink in Copy Activity. Configure a Cosmos DB linked service (connection string or managed identity). As a source: configure a dataset pointing to a database/container; optionally add a query filter for incremental loads using a lastModified watermark pattern. As a sink: configure write behavior (insert, upsert). ADF's parallel copy (degree of copy parallelism, DIUs) scales throughput. For initial bulk loads, increase Cosmos DB RU temporarily and ADF's DIUs together. For incremental loads, use a pipeline parameter for the high-water mark and store it in a control table (Azure SQL or Cosmos DB itself). Synapse pipelines are built on ADF and share the same Cosmos DB connector; use Synapse when the pipeline is part of a broader analytics workspace.
fj: Azure Data Factory can read from the Cosmos DB change feed natively without any custom coding.
fs: ADF's Cosmos DB source connector reads via point-in-time queries and offset-based pagination, not the change feed API. To process the change feed with ADF, you would need a custom activity (Azure Function). Native change feed processing is done via Azure Functions with Cosmos DB trigger or the SDK change feed processor.

q: How do you use the Kafka connector to move data into Azure Cosmos DB?
a: The Azure Cosmos DB Kafka Connector (Apache Kafka Connect plugin) enables streaming data from Kafka topics into Cosmos DB (sink connector) or from Cosmos DB change feed to Kafka topics (source connector). Configuration: deploy Kafka Connect workers, install the Cosmos DB connector jar (from Confluent Hub or GitHub), and configure a connector JSON with: connector.class = com.azure.cosmos.kafka.connect.CosmosDBSinkConnector, connect.cosmos.connection.endpoint, connect.cosmos.master.key, connect.cosmos.databasename, connect.cosmos.containers.topicmap (Kafka topic → Cosmos DB container mapping), tasks.max. The connector handles batching, retries on 429s, and at-least-once delivery. For the source connector (change feed → Kafka), the connector wraps the change feed processor. This pattern is ideal for event-driven architectures where Cosmos DB must participate in a Kafka ecosystem without custom code.
fj: The Cosmos DB Kafka Connector is a Microsoft-proprietary connector available only through Azure Event Hubs.
fs: The Cosmos DB Kafka Connector is an open-source Apache Kafka Connect plugin available on GitHub (Azure/kafka-connect-cosmosdb) and Confluent Hub. It works with any Kafka deployment (Confluent Cloud, self-hosted, Azure HDInsight Kafka, Azure Event Hubs with Kafka protocol) — not exclusive to Azure Event Hubs.

q: How do you stream data into Cosmos DB using Azure Stream Analytics?
a: Configure a Stream Analytics job with an input source (IoT Hub, Event Hubs, Blob Storage) and a Cosmos DB output. In the job output settings: select 'Cosmos DB' as output type, provide the account name, key, database name, container name, and a document ID column (a field from the input data that becomes the Cosmos DB item id, ideally the partition key or a combination). The Stream Analytics job processes streaming data in near real-time and writes each event as a document to Cosmos DB. Write mode: update (upsert by id) or insert (always create). Use the partitionKey property in output config to route each write to the correct logical partition. Stream Analytics scales write throughput with streaming units; increase SUs for high-volume streams. Ideal for IoT telemetry, clickstream, and real-time event persistence to Cosmos DB.
fj: Azure Stream Analytics can only write to Cosmos DB containers that have an analytical store enabled.
fs: Stream Analytics writes to the Cosmos DB transactional store regardless of whether the analytical store is enabled. The analytical store is a separate feature for analytical queries; it has no effect on Stream Analytics output configuration or write behavior.

q: How do you use the Azure Cosmos DB Spark connector to move data at scale?
a: The Cosmos DB Spark connector (azure-cosmos-spark, available on Maven and Databricks Libraries) reads from and writes to the Cosmos DB transactional store using the Spark DataFrame API. Read: spark.read.format('cosmos.oltp').options({...}).load(). Write: df.write.format('cosmos.oltp').options({...}).mode('append').save(). Key options: spark.cosmos.write.bulk.enabled = true (batch writes efficiently), spark.cosmos.read.partitioning.strategy = Restrictive|Default|Aggressive (controls how Spark partitions map to Cosmos DB partitions), spark.cosmos.throughputControl.enabled = true with a target throughput group to cap RU usage during migration. This connector is ideal for large-scale migrations from Data Lake Gen2, existing Spark pipelines, or Databricks workloads. Transformations (schema changes, denormalization, type coercion) can be applied using standard Spark DataFrame operations before writing.
fj: The Cosmos DB Spark connector reads from the analytical store by default for all Spark workloads.
fs: The Cosmos DB Spark connector for OLTP (cosmos.oltp format) reads from and writes to the transactional store. The Cosmos DB Spark connector for OLAP (cosmos.olap format, used with Synapse Link) reads from the analytical store. They are distinct formats — OLTP reads consume RU from the container; OLAP reads do not.

q: How do you configure Azure Cosmos DB as a custom endpoint for Azure IoT Hub to store device telemetry?
a: IoT Hub supports custom endpoints (message routing) to send device-to-cloud messages to a Cosmos DB container. Configuration in the portal: IoT Hub → Message routing → Custom endpoints → Add endpoint → select 'Cosmos DB'. Provide the Cosmos DB account, database, container name, and authentication method (connection string or managed identity). Define a partition key name (the field in the message body or system properties to use as the Cosmos DB partition key). Optionally enable synthetic partition key by combining multiple fields. Then create a Message Route with source 'Device messages', condition (filter), and the Cosmos DB endpoint as destination. Messages matching the route are written directly to Cosmos DB by IoT Hub without requiring an Azure Function or Stream Analytics job. Throughput on the Cosmos DB container must be sufficient for the IoT ingestion rate.
fj: IoT Hub can only route messages to Azure Storage, Event Hubs, and Service Bus — Cosmos DB is not a supported custom endpoint.
fs: Azure IoT Hub added Cosmos DB as a supported custom routing endpoint, allowing direct device telemetry ingestion into Cosmos DB without an intermediate compute layer. This reduces architecture complexity and end-to-end latency for IoT persistence scenarios.

q: How do you provision and manage Azure Cosmos DB resources using ARM templates?
a: ARM templates define Cosmos DB resources using types: Microsoft.DocumentDB/databaseAccounts (account), /databaseAccounts/sqlDatabases (database), /sqlDatabases/containers (container). Key properties on the account: locations array (regions + failover priorities), defaultConsistencyLevel, enableAutomaticFailover, enableMultipleWriteLocations, keyVaultKeyUri (CMK). On containers: partitionKey paths and kind (Hash or MultiHash for hierarchical), indexingPolicy, defaultTtl, throughput or autoscaleSettings. Deploy via az deployment group create or Azure DevOps pipeline. ARM templates enable repeatable, version-controlled infrastructure. Use ARM template functions (concat, resourceId) to wire up dependencies. Bicep is the preferred declarative syntax that compiles to ARM JSON and is more readable. Properties like throughput mode (standard vs autoscale) and CMK are set at creation — some cannot be changed post-deploy.
fj: You can use an ARM template to migrate an existing Cosmos DB container from standard to autoscale throughput.
fs: ARM templates do not support migrating between manual and autoscale throughput on existing resources — this is a limitation of the resource provider. Throughput mode migration must be done via the portal, CLI, or PowerShell. ARM templates can set the throughput configuration at creation but cannot change the mode post-creation.

code.label: ARM template snippet: Cosmos DB container with autoscale

q: How do you migrate a Cosmos DB container between standard (manual) and autoscale throughput using PowerShell or Azure CLI?
a: Migration between standard and autoscale throughput is a one-click or one-command operation with no downtime. Via CLI to migrate to autoscale: az cosmosdb sql container throughput migrate --account-name myaccount --database-name mydb --name mycontainer --resource-group myRG --throughput-type autoscale. To migrate back to manual: use --throughput-type manual. Via PowerShell: Invoke-AzCosmosDBSqlContainerThroughputMigration -AccountName myaccount -DatabaseName mydb -Name mycontainer -ResourceGroupName myRG -ThroughputType Autoscale. When migrating to autoscale, Cosmos DB sets the max RU/s to the current manual RU/s value (minimum 4000 RU/s). When migrating from autoscale to manual, it sets RU/s to the current autoscale max. These commands also work at the database level for shared throughput databases.
fj: Migrating from autoscale to manual throughput requires recreating the container to apply the new billing model.
fs: Throughput mode migration is an in-place, non-destructive operation that does not require container recreation or data migration. It updates the billing model for the existing container immediately. Data, indexes, and partitioning are unchanged.

code.label: Migrate container to autoscale via Azure CLI
lines: az cosmosdb sql container throughput migrate \

q: How do you initiate a regional failover programmatically using PowerShell or Azure CLI?
a: Manual failover changes the write region priority order. Via CLI: az cosmosdb failover-priority-change --account-name myaccount --resource-group myRG --failover-policies '[{"locationName":"East US 2","failoverPriority":0},{"locationName":"West US","failoverPriority":1}]'. The region with failoverPriority 0 becomes the new write region. Via PowerShell: Update-AzCosmosDBAccountFailoverPriority -ResourceGroupName myRG -Name myaccount -FailoverPolicy @{LocationName='East US 2';FailoverPriority=0},{LocationName='West US';FailoverPriority=1}). Note: for this manual priority change to work, automatic failover must be disabled on the account. For DR drill purposes, use the portal's 'Manual Failover' button which temporarily makes the account read-only during the switch. Applications using the global endpoint and SDK preferred regions automatically discover the new write region without code changes.
fj: A manual regional failover via CLI immediately reroutes all active application connections to the new write region.
fs: The failover changes the write region metadata at the Cosmos DB account level. Active application connections using the SDK do not instantly reroute — the SDK refreshes its partition routing map periodically (every ~5 minutes by default) or on the next request failure. Applications may briefly route writes to the old region before discovering the new write region. Using the global endpoint with ApplicationPreferredRegions minimizes this window.

code.label: Initiate manual regional failover via CLI
